CZ_NAME: LOS ANGELES COUNTY MOUNTAINS EXCLUDING THE SANTA MONICA RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface high pressure over the Great Basin generated strong and gusty north to northeast winds across the mountains of Ventura and Los Angeles counties. Wind gusts up to 79 MPH were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong north to northeast winds developed across the mountains of Los Angeles county. Some wind reports from local RAWS stations include Chilao (gusts 59 to 73 MPH), Mill Creek (gusts 60-74 MPH) and Camp Nine (gusts 59 to 78 MPH).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shallow marine layer resulted in areas of dense fog along the coast, mainly in San Diego County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brown Field municipal airport ASOS reported one-quarter mile visibility from 2153 PST on the 19th through 0616 PST on the 20th. Montgomery Field ASOS reported mostly one-quarter mile visibility from 2317 PST on the 19th through 0620 PST on the 20th. Carlsbad McClellan-Palomar airport ASOS reported mostly one-quarter mile visibility between 0010-0620 PST on the 20th. Camp Pendleton MCAS ASOS reported mostly one-quarter mile visibility between 0154-0803 PST on the 20th. A spotter in San Marcos reported visibility of only 100 feet with a fog depth of around 50 feet around 0730 PST on the 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface high pressure area over the Great Basin resulted in gusty east to northeast winds near the mountain crests, in the coastal foothills, and western inland valleys, including the Inland Empire. Gusty winds began on the 21st, reaching peak strength on the 23rd through 24th. Several stations recorded gusts above 65 mph. Damage included several trees and power lines down in the Inland Empire and foothills of San Diego County. There were also reports of two semi trucks overturned, one in Rancho Cucamonga, and the other just west of Pine Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred at the Fremont Canyon RAWS between 0000-2200 PST on the 24th. During this time, winds were sustained out of the ENE at 35-40 mph with gusts in the 60s and 70s. A peak gust of 77 mph was recorded between 0651-0751 PST. High winds occurred at the Pleasants Peak mesonet between 0222-1352 PST on the 24th. During this time, winds were sustained out of the ENE at 40-50 mph with gusts in the upper 50s and 60s. A peak gust of 63 mph was recorded twice between 0852-0952 PST.

CZ_NAME: IMPERIAL COUNTY EXCEPT THE LOWER COLORADO RIVER VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and cold upper level low pressure system and associated cold front moved across far southeast California through the New Year's holiday period and ushered in freezing temperatures to Imperial county early in January 2015. Freezing or sub freezing low temperatures affected the Imperial valley beginning on January 1st and the cold temperatures persisted through the 3rd of the month. The cold temperatures prompted the issuance of multiple Freeze Warnings for Imperial county and they resulted in damage to both crops as well as ornamental and decorative vegetation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very cold upper level low pressure system, and associated cold front, ushered in freezing temperatures to the deserts of Imperial county beginning on January 1st. According to the official ASOS weather station at Imperial, the low temperature on January 1st was 27 degrees. A Freeze Warning was in effect for the morning hours on this day. The cold temperatures resulted in damage to crops as well as ornamental and decorative plants across the Imperial valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous Pacific low pressure system spread copious amounts of moisture northward and into southeastern California during the morning hours on January 30th. As a result, widespread showers occurred over eastern Riverside county, and they produced locally heavy rainfall. Isolated embedded thunderstorms enhanced the locally heavy rainfall totals. The heavy rain led to flooding just west of the lower Colorado River valley, between Blythe and Parker. The flooding led to the closure of highway 95 in multiple locations, due to high water and rock debris on the road. No injuries were reported as a result of the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A vigorous Pacific low pressure system spread copious amounts of moisture northward and into far southeast California during the morning hours on January 30th. This resulted in widespread showers and isolated thunderstorms, locally heavy at times. The heavy rain led to flooding along Highway 95 north of Blythe. According to local emergency managers, at 0930PST flooding led to the closure of Highway 95 at 2nd Avenue, located approximately 5 miles north of Blythe. No accidents or injuries were reported due to the flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A vigorous Pacific low pressure system spread copious moisture northward and into far southeast California during the morning hours on January 30th. This resulted in widespread showers and isolated thunderstorms across eastern Riverside county. Rainfall was locally heavy at times and as a result, flooding occurred just west of the lower Colorado River valley, between Parker and Blythe. According to local law enforcement, at 0930PST flooding resulted in rock debris across Highway 95 and this forced the closure of the highway at Agnes Wilson Road, approximately 7 miles northwest of Poston. No accidents or injuries were reported due to the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of disturbances moved into Southern California on January 10 and 11, 2015, and brushed across Kern county and the Tulare county portion of the Sierra Nevada. Only light precipitation occurred on January 10, but the system that moved through on the 11th was stronger and produced locally heavy rain. This resulted in some street flooding in the Bakersfield area along with some rock slides along Highway 178 in the Kern county mountains, and along Redrock-Randsburg Road in the Kern county desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in a rock slide on the eastbound side of Highway 178 near the mouth of the Kern Canyon. The road was blocked by baseball sized rocks.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong north to northeast winds developed across the mountains of Ventura county. Wind gusts in excess of 70 MPH were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong upper level low pressure system produced showers and thunderstorms across the lower elevations of southeast California during the day on May 15th. The wet conditions allowed for the formation of patchy dense fog along the lower Colorado River Valley during the morning hours on May 16th; dense fog was reported at the airport at Blythe California. No accidents or injuries were reported due to the sharply reduced visibilities in dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong upper level low pressure system led to wet conditions along the lower Colorado River valley during the morning hours on May 16th. As a result, patchy and locally dense fog developed which affected the airport at Blythe California. At 0552PST the ASOS weather station at Blythe reported visibility reduced to one quarter of a mile. No accidents or injuries were reported due to the dense fog. A Dense Fog Advisory was issued for the area due to the reported low visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface high pressure over the Great Basin generated strong and gusty north to northeast winds across the mountains of Ventura and Los Angeles counties. Wind gusts up to 79 MPH were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong north to northeast winds developed across the Santa Monica Mountains. The RAWS sensor at Malibu Hills reported wind gusts between 58 and 76 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough deepened along the West Coast before moving inland over Southern California and brought rain and snow showers, as well as a few thunderstorms with hail, through the morning of the March 3rd. Huntington Beach reported around one inch of hail accumulation during the morning of the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trace to 5 inches of snow was reported from 0000 PST on the 1st through 0700 PST on the 2nd across the area. The heaviest total of 5 inches was reported by a spotter 2 mi ESE of Big Bear City. Additional snowfall amounts of 4 inches in Forest Falls, 3 inches in Angelus Oaks, and 2 inches in Green Valley Lake were also reported. Multiple traffic collisions were reported in the mountain top district, especially along Highway 38 between Angelus Oaks and Hatchery Drive (Big Bear). The aforementioned section of Highway 38 was closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough deepened along the West Coast before moving inland over Southern California and brought rain and snow showers, as well as a few thunderstorms with hail, through the morning of the March 3rd. Huntington Beach reported around one inch of hail accumulation during the morning of the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a brief cold air funnel over Perris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough deepened along the West Coast before moving inland over Southern California and brought rain and snow showers, as well as a few thunderstorms with hail, through the morning of the March 3rd. Huntington Beach reported around one inch of hail accumulation during the morning of the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms dropped up to 1 inch depth accumulation of 0.5-in diameter hail on Huntington Beach. The hail covered the beach near Huntington Beach Pier and numerous roads within the city of Huntington Beach. The event received widespread attention from local and national media and extensive social media photos.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm caused damage and flooding around Montague and Grenada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported widespread tree damage on County road A12 from near Montague into Grenada with numerous 2-3 inch limbs down.  He also reported pea-sized hail and minor street flooding.  Wind gusts were estimated at 40-50 mph, but damage indicates they may have been higher.   Another spotter 4W Grenada reported 1.5 inches of rain in less than 90 minutes along with 0.75 inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains associated with a severe thunderstorm caused flooding in and around Grenada California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yreka Dispatch reported at 20/1955 PST that homes were flooded in Grenada and that the Shepherd Of God Assisted Living Center was being evacuated due to water entering the building.  CHP reported standing water and debris near the I-5 southbound on-ramp at 20/1919 PST along with lots of debris on the southbound side of Old Highway 99.  At 20/1925 PST, they reported that the on-ramp was blocked by flooding.  At 20/1932 PST debris was reported across both lanes of county road A12. A spotter 4W of Grenada reported 1.50 inches of rain in less than 90 minutes ending at 20/1924 PST.  The same spotter reported 0.75 inch hail at 20/1810 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains associated with strong thunderstorms caused flooding in and around McCloud California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Siskiyou County Sheriff's Department reported Flash Flooding in the Mud Creek Basin northeast of McCloud at 21/2020 PST.  They also reported Flash Flooding in the city of McCloud due to runoff from heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak low pressure dropped into northern California on the 25th, aiding the development of scattered thunderstorms with strong winds and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind measured between 1245 and 1345PST, with the time of the gust estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface high in the wake of a departing trough resulted in strong easterly winds over San Diego County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred at the Sill Hill mesonet between 2000 PST on the 5th and 0500 PST on the 6th. During this time, winds were sustained out of the east at 35-45 mph with most gusts in the high 50s to mid 60s mph. However, a peak gust of 71 mph was recorded between 2130 and 2140 PST on the 5th. Strong winds were also reported at the Big Black Mountain mesonet station from 0627 to 0747 PST on the 6th, with a peak gust of 68 mph. Winds were more tame across the rest of the zone, with other mesonet stations reporting gusts from the 30s to low 50s mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm over Modoc County brought strong winds to the area on the evening|of 7/09/15.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Juniper Creek RAWS recorded a gust to 67 mph sometime during this hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough deepened along the West Coast before moving inland over Southern California and brought rain and snow showers, as well as a few thunderstorms with hail, through the morning of the March 3rd. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters in Idyllwild and Pine Cove both reported a 24 hour snowfall accumulation of 9.5 inches as of 0800 PST on the 2nd. A third spotter in Pine Cove reported 8 inches of snowfall in 12 hours, as of 2100 PST on the 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough deepened along the West Coast before moving inland over Southern California and brought rain and snow showers, as well as a few thunderstorms with hail, through the morning of the March 3rd. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of rain and thunderstorms moved over the Silverado Burn scar between 1045 and 1200 PST on the 2nd, producing rain rates in excess of 0.5 inches per 30 minutes. As a result, a moderate mud flow occurred in the backyard of a local resident. Video was shared with NWS San Diego.

CZ_NAME: LOS ANGELES COUNTY MOUNTAINS EXCLUDING THE SANTA MONICA RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A extended period of strong and gusty west to northwest winds impacted parts of Southern California. Across the Antelope Valley, strong westerly winds, gusting to 64 MPH, were reported. In the mountains of Santa Barbara, Ventura and Los Angeles counties, strong northwest to north winds, gusting to 65 MPH, were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The RAWS sensor at Camp Nine reported west to northwest wind gusts between 58 and 60 MPH. The RAWS sensor at Chilao reported sustained westerly winds of 42 to 44 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of lingering tropical moisture from former hurricane Dolores and a weather disturbance moving into southern California led to intense thunderstorms with torrential rainfall across portions of eastern Riverside county during the afternoon hours on July 19th. A cluster of slow moving thunderstorms near and just to the west of Desert Center produced more than 6 inches of rain along Interstate 10; the heavy rain caused significant flash flooding which washed out the eastbound lanes of the freeway. At this location, the interstate crossed a wash, and the flowing water raging through the wash collapsed the bridge across it. One vehicle drove into the wash; fortunately the driver was rescued and there were no fatalities. In addition, the flooding also eroded the westbound lanes of the freeway and as a result, Interstate 10 was closed indefinitely in both directions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Aided by lingering tropical moisture from former hurricane Dolores, intense thunderstorms with torrential rain developed near Desert Center during the afternoon hours on July 19th. Based on radar estimates, over 6 inches of rain fell over several hours along and just to the south of Interstate 10, just to the west of Desert Center. According to a trained weather spotter about 4 miles west of Desert Center, the eastbound lanes of Interstate 10 collapsed where they crossed a heavily flowing wash, and one vehicle drove into the hole in the collapsed bridge. The driver became trapped by crushed asphalt and needed to be rescued. The injured driver was extracted from his truck and taken to a local hospital. According to local law enforcement, Interstate 10 was closed in both directions; water was flowing over the highway and the on and off ramps were flooded. Traffic was backed up for miles and needed to be re-routed. Within a short distance of the collapsed bridge, water flowing through a wash and under the freeway severely eroded and compromised the integrity of a second bridge; the second bridge did not collapse however. Interstate 10 will be closed in both directions for quite some time, probably at least one week, as bridges on both sides of the freeway will need to be re-built. A Flash Flood Warning was issued for the area from 1621PST through 1915PST.

CZ_NAME: COASTAL NORTH BAY INCLUDING POINT REYES NATIONAL SEASHORE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large ocean swell were breaking along the Northern California Coast on December 23, 2015.  In addition, moderately strong northwest winds were observed over the coastal waters.  These conditions contributed to the death of a kayaker at the mouth of the Russian River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A kayaker drowned after he encountered high surf at the mouth of the Russian River near Goat Rock State Beach on the Sonoma County Coast. The kayaker launched with one other individual into the Russian River near Jenner. Once he encountered the large surf, his kayak overturned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty west to northwest winds developed behind the departing weather system during the early morning hours of Tuesday, December 22. Wind gusts of between 40 and 50 mph were common, especially along the coast and in the hills, and local wind gusts in excess of 60 mph occurred in the North Bay Mountains. Thousands of power outages were reported due to these strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts at the Big Rock RAWS in Marin County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts reported by the Atlas Peak RAWS in Napa County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high surf event occurred along the northern and central California coast on December 10 and 11. Peak wave heights at coastal buoys from Point Arena south to Point Sur occurred between 7 pm and midnight on the evening of December 10, but very large waves continued to break along the coast through the following day. Peak swell heights recorded at coastal buoys ranged from 24 to 33 feet. An offshore buoy 250 miles west of San Francisco reported a peak swell height of 35 feet. The 30 foot swell recorded by the Monterey Bay Buoy at 11 pm on December 10 fell short of the record swell height at that buoy which is 32 feet set on January 5, 2008.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New Brighton State Beach and Seacliff State Beach in Santa Cruz county were closed to vehicle access due to heavy surf. The surf was coming up over walls along parking lots in many places.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large breaking waves sent water across Ocean View Boulevard in Pacific Grove on the morning of December 11.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The long term drought continues as May was yet another below normal month for precipitation for much of the area. There was some mountain precipitation in the form of rain, but much of it was focused along and east of the crest. Snow pack was at the lowest levels on record and by the end of the month was virtually nonexistent. As a result, reservoirs across the area by the end of the month were at well below normal levels and were already beginning to drop. ||Extreme drought was detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor across most the region, with exceptional drought for the northern San Joaquin Valley, the central and southern Sacramento Valley, the Burney Basin and the Sierra south of Interstate 80. ||Governor Jerry Brown declared a drought emergency for the entire state of California January 17, 2014 and this continued to be in effect. ||As a result of continuing drought conditions, emergency legislation appropriated over $1 billion of additional funds for drought-related projects and activities.|The Administration���s May Revision proposal includes an additional $2.2 billion for programs that protect and expand local water supplies, improve water conservation, and provide immediate relief to impacted communities. ||Local Emergency Proclamations have been issued for El Dorado, Glenn, Lake, Plumas, San Joaquin, Shasta, Sutter, Tuolumne, and Yuba counties. The cities of Live Oak Lodi, and Ripon continue in a drought emergency. Drought task forces have been established in Butte, Lake, Nevada, Placer, Plumas, Sacramento, San Joaquin, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tehama, Tuolumne, and Yolo counties to coordinate response to the drought. ||Mandatory water reductions of 25 percent as ordered by Governor Brown were in effect statewide, the first time such mandatory restrictions have been enacted. ||Agriculture has been heavily impacted, with numerous farm and farm related jobs lost. Spring planting is being scaled back significantly, with many existing tree crops and vines being removed due to a lack of irrigation water. The state continued to supply food assistance to those impacted. California Disaster Assistance Act money continued to be provided for those who have lost drinking water due to dry wells. ||Construction was begun of a $40 million temporary drought barrier on the West False River in the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta was started to keep tidal salt water from contaminating drinking water to Bay Area residents.||Additional impacts included low spring runoff on fisheries and ecological affects on forests and wildlife.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Agriculture continued to be impacted with reduced planting and harvest of spring crops, bringing a significant loss of jobs. Tree mortality across the Valley increased, including  even normally drought resistant oak trees. Golf courses faced pressure to use less water and were doing so, dry, brown appearance of the turf. Some golf courses shut down for lack of water. Many people reduced landscape watering at homes or removed grass lawns.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very large and cold upper-level low deepened along the West Coast before moving inland directly over southern California. This late-season system brought significant rain and snow showers over and west of the mountains on the 7th and 8th. Most of these areas saw between one-quarter of an inch and one inch of precipitation over these two days. The snow level dropped to around 3500 feet with light snow falling, but not sticking at that elevation. Above 4000 feet, snow amounts ranged from 1 to 6 inches, requiring chain control on Sunrise Highway in San Diego County. Flooding due to heavy rainfall was reported at the Spring Valley Quarry Road dip and Escondido Creek on Country Club Drive, where the roads were closed. At the beginning of the event, strong westerly winds produced gusts to 60 mph at Edom Hill and 65 mph at Whitewater RAWS, both in the Banning Pass/mountain desert slope region of Riverside County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CHP reported Country Club Drive closed due to water flowing over road at dip.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowfall exceeding 8-14 inches caused travel problems in the mountains, with some light accumulations reported down to elevations around 2500 feet. Thunderstorms locally brought both large hail and large amounts of small hail, causing traffic accidents and spin outs. Multiple funnel clouds were reported, but there were no confirmed touchdowns. Heavy rainfall of 1 to 2 inches was measured, with some ponding on roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Caltrans measured a total of 8 inches of snow at Kingvale at 6100 ft. Up to 14 inches of snow was measured at Echo Summit. This snow brought chain restrictions, accidents, and slow traffic on I80 and Highway 50. Small hail from thunderstorms caused travel accidents along Highway 49.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak pacific front moved through Southern California on the evening of April 7 through early morning on April 8th, producing light snowfall in the higher elevations of the San Bernardino County Mountains. Rainfall of 0.25 to 0.50 inches occurred in northern Orange County and across the slopes of the mountains in the Cajon Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported (via social media) 2 to 3 inches of snowfall in 12 hours in the Upper Moonridge area of Big Bear.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system from the Pacific northwest dropped through the region bringing showers and thunderstorms, with up to 7 inches of new snow over the Sierra.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow caused slippery roads on mountains and passes above 6500 feet, with 7 inches of snow measured 3W of Shriner Lake Campground. This impacted mountain roads from Highway 50 southward, including Echo Summit on Highway 50, Carson Pass on Highway 88, Ebbetts Pass on Highway 4, and Sonora Pass on Highway 104.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold, late-season upper-level low moved inland over southern California on the 14th and 15th, bringing another round of winter storm-like conditions and precipitation. The initial round of rainfall occurred the afternoon of the 14th, heaviest in Orange County and the Inland Empire. Additional intense cells developed (with thunder) between 0500-0700 PST from Oceanside to Temecula and Point Loma to El Cajon. Lindbergh Field received 1.3 inches in 1 hour and 0.71 in 9 minutes during this time, setting records. Impacts included a debris flow near Fallbrook along Highway 395, 6 high water rescues reported by Lifeguards from Midway to Mission Hills and North Park, and flooding/road closure again at Spring Valley Quarry Road dip and Escondido Creek on Country Club Drive. The Padres baseball game experienced a rare 2-hour delay as well, due to the rain. The Fashion Valley river gage on the San Diego River reached 7 feet with road closures and golf course flooding in the area. A flash flood watch was in effect for the 15th due to the widespread showers in recently burned areas and steep terrain. A flash flood warning was issued on the 14th for southwestern San Diego County due to a thunderstorm with heavy rain.|In addition to the rain, winds were very strong, gusting in the upper 50s and 60s mph below the E-W passes and along the desert mountain slopes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were reports of San Diego fire and police responding to multiple (6 incidents) swift water rescues in the Midway and Mission Hills neighborhoods.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms caused isolated flash flooding in the Mojave Desert.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Lake Fire began on June 17th around 1440 PST on Jenks Lake Road West near Camp de Benneville Pines in the eastern San Bernardino Mountains. The fire burned rapidly south and east near Highway 38 south of Big Bear toward the Morongo Valley in heavy timber.  Evacuations: 120 people from Camp de Benneville Pines, 1 person from Camp Ta Ta Pochon, 24 people from Camp Alpine Meadows, 23 people from Camp Edwards, and 10 people from Camp Tulakes. Mandatory evacuations were in place from east of Angelus Oaks to Onyx Summit on both sides of Hwy 38. All Hiking Trails into the San Gorgonio Wilderness Area were closed due to the proximity of the fire to many hiking trails. The Pacific Crest Trail (PCT) was closed from Whitewater Preserve to Onyx Summit. Highway 38 was closed between Angelus Oaks to Lake Williams. Jenks Lake Road was closed as well. The Mojave Desert Air Quality Management District issued a Smoke Advisory for Yucca Valley, Morongo Valley, Lucerne Valley and surrounding communities. The Lake Fire burned a total of 31,359 acres before being fully contained around 2115 PST on August 1. The fire was human-caused, but still under investigation. Approximately 10 structures were damaged or destroyed, including one home and 3 outbuildings.

While quite a few mountain locations received greater than normal precipitation due to moisture from the monsoon and from ex-hurricane Dolores, this made little impact on the drought overall. The main affects were in decreasing fire activity in areas where locally heavy rain fell. Without a snow pack, reservoirs across the area by the end of the month were continuing to drop well below normal levels.||Extreme drought continued to be detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor across most the region, with exceptional drought for the northern San Joaquin Valley, the central and southern Sacramento Valley, the Burney Basin and the Sierra. ||The warm, dry winter left vegetation parched and ready to burn. Wildfire activity was well above normal levels, with California experiencing 4,201 wildfires that consumed 100,000 acres through August 1, much higher than the five-year average of 2,729 fires burning 48,153 acres, according to the California Department of Forestry and Fire Protection. The Rocky Fire alone burned 69,000 acres in Lake, Yolo and Colusa counties and destroyed 43 homes and 53 outbuildings as of August 6. Additional fast burning wildfires include the Wragg, Lowell, and Kyburz fires.||Governor Jerry Brown declared a drought emergency for the entire state of California January 17, 2014 and this continued to be in effect. ||Local Emergency Proclamations have been issued for El Dorado, Glenn, Lake, Plumas, San Joaquin, Shasta, Sutter, Tuolumne, and Yuba counties. The cities of Live Oak Lodi, and Ripon continue in a drought emergency. Drought task forces have been established in Butte, Lake, Nevada, Placer, Plumas, Sacramento, San Joaquin, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tehama, Tuolumne, and Yolo counties to coordinate response to the drought. ||Mandatory water reductions of 25 percent as ordered by Governor Brown continue to be in effect statewide, the first time such mandatory restrictions have been enacted. ||Agriculture has been heavily impacted, with numerous farm and farm related jobs lost. The state continued to supply food assistance to those impacted. California Disaster Assistance Act money continued to be provided for those who have lost drinking water due to dry wells. ||Additional impacts included low runoff in streams and rivers on fish and ecological affects on forests and wildlife.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually late-season low pressure system combined with unusually early-season deep subtropical moisture to fuel scattered thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Some storms produced flash flooding, and isolated wind damage also occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The long term drought continued through June with yet another below normal month for precipitation for much of the area. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Construction of a $40 million temporary drought barrier on the West False River in the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta was completed. This barrier is used to keep tidal salt water from contaminating drinking water to Bay Area residents. The State Water Resources Control Board has a program that allowed riparian water right holders in the Delta to voluntarily cut back water use by 25% in exchange for assurances they would not face deeper curtailments during the June-September growing season.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moving north through extreme eastern Lassen County brought severe outflow winds in the evening on the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts between 59 and 74 mph were recorded at the Bull Flat RAWS between 1740PST and 1940PST. Time of severe gusts are approximate and based on radar signatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Breezy southwest winds combined with dry fuels to intensify the development of wildfires, including the Kyburz and Wragg Fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fire covered 75 acres. A vehicle fire started the fire east of Whitehall, which spread to both sides of US Highway 50. The vehicle that started the fire was destroyed, and there was one minor injury to a firefighter. Highway 50 was shut down through the rest of the afternoon into the next day. There were 15 people in the area evacuated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed, mainly in the mountains. These brought periods of heavy rain with local flooding and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree blown down on mobile home in the town of Shasta Lake, causing significant damage.

Dry northwest winds enhanced wildfires, including the Rocky Fire in Lake County and a property damaging grass fire in Elverta, in northern Sacramento County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fire started Monday afternoon along Browning Street, about two miles north of Elverta Road, off Highway 99 and Baseline Road in Elverta, Sacramento County. The fire spread into Placer County. One home, six cars and a tractor were destroyed by the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry northwest winds enhanced wildfires, including the Rocky Fire in Lake County and a property damaging grass fire in Elverta, in northern Sacramento County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rocky Fire burned 18,000 acres by the end of July, and caused the evacuation of 650 people. The fire ultimately burned 68,000 acres by August 14, with 43 residences, 53 outbuildings destroyed; 8 structures damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough developed along the central California coast and dropped south before moving inland over the high desert of southern California during the afternoon. This produced scattered showers and thunderstorms over the San Bernardino Mountains and high desert with some locally intense rainfall and small hail. Rainfall amounts were generally one-tenth to one-third of an inch with a few higher totals in Phelan (1.20 inches) and Antelope Creek (0.47 inches). Minor flooding was reported in Phelan and Wrightwood with a few rock and debris slides along Highways 2 and 138. Two urban/small stream flood advisories were issued for the high desert during this event. One-half inch hail was reported near Wrightwood, and one-quarter inch hail near Lucern Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CHP reported water build-up, north of Apple Valley, at the Dale Evans Parkway offramp from I-15 causing vehicles to slide. CHP also reported rock and mud slides into the slow lane, which was shut down near Phelan, as well as Highway 18 flooded west of Victorville with debris in the roadway. A resident near Baldy Mesa reported debris at the junction of Highways 138 and 2.

Drought task forces have been established in Butte, Lake, Nevada, Placer, Plumas, Sacramento, San Joaquin, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tehama, Tuolumne, and Yolo counties to coordinate response to the drought. ||Mandatory water reductions of 25 percent as ordered by Governor Brown continue to be in effect statewide, the first time such mandatory restrictions have been enacted. ||Agriculture has been heavily impacted, with numerous farm and farm related jobs lost. The state continued to supply food assistance to those impacted. California Disaster Assistance Act money continued to be provided for those who have lost drinking water due to dry wells. ||Additional impacts included low runoff in streams and rivers on fish and ecological affects on forests and wildlife.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Agriculture continued to be impacted in July bringing a significant loss of jobs. Tree mortality across the Valley increased, including even normally drought resistant oak trees. Golf courses faced pressure to use less water and were doing so, bringing a dry, brown appearance to the turf. Some golf courses shut down for lack of water. Many people reduced landscape watering at homes or removed grass lawns.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continues in full force during the month of July, 2015. Temperatures continued to average above normal. July was a good month for precipitation as there were multiple monsoonal and tropical influxes of moisture into the area. However, this wasn't nearly enough to impact the drought situation. Rainfall totals for Fresno received 0.43 of an inch of rain, while Bakersfield received 0.04 inches. ||The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The 2013-2014 water year (July 1 - June 30) concluded with Fresno setting its second driest on record (4.81 inches) and Bakersfield setting its third driest (2.41 inches). For the calendar year, 2014 was the warmest year on record for Fresno and Bakersfield. The general trend of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures has continued this year. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, a decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the Southern Sierra Nevada, reduction in air quality due to persistent stagnant air, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. Also, communities in Central California interior face state mandated reductions in municipal water use.
Low pressure over the Pacific Northwest on the 27th dropped south through California on the 28th, bringing heavy snow to the Lake Tahoe area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ski areas and an avalanche forecaster (Sierra Avalanche Center) around the Lake Tahoe area reported 14 to 28 inches of snowfall, with the highest amounts near the crest. At lake level snow amounts varied greatly, with a spotter near Meyers indicating 13 inches but many other areas around the lake and near Truckee only reporting between 5 and 9 inches.

The Cabin fire was started by lightning on July 19, 2015 and continued to burn through then entire month of August. The fire was 96% contained as of September 1, and was burning into the footprint of the 2011 Lion Fire. It was located 8 mile northeast of Camp Nelson in Sequoia National Forest. The fire remained around 2 acres in size until July 30, 2015, then became active and burned 6980 acres by the end of August. There were no structures lost and no fatalities. Cost of containment was $7.7 million as of September 1, 2015.

The Rough Fire was started by lightning on July 31, 2015 in the Kings River Drainage, 5 miles north of Hume Lake and 2.5 miles southwest of Spanish Mountain. The fire burned actively through August and continued into September. As of September 2, it had burned 79,963 acres. The fire was burning in the Sierra National Forest, Sequoia National Forest, and Sequoia & Kings Canyon National Parks. The fire closed Highway 180 east of Grant Grove and closed all of Kings Canyon National Park. The fire threatened infrastructure in the Hume Lake area as well as in Cedar Grove and several other small communities. There were 5 structures lost. The cost of containment as of September 2, was 57.8 million.

Thunderstorms developed across northwestern Riverside county, including Joshua Tree National Park, during the early afternoon hours on August 26th. Some of the stronger storms had the potential to produce winds in excess of 60 mph and initially, Severe Thunderstorm Warnings were issued for the park. Shortly thereafter the storms dropped very heavy rainfall which especially impacted the northern portions of the park. Two flash floods were reported within a short time, one of which produced debris flow across the road just one mile from the north park entrance. The road was closed due to the debris. A Flash Flood Warning was in effect when the flash flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across portions of Joshua Tree National Park during the early afternoon hours on August 26th, and some of the stronger ones produced very intense rainfall which quickly led to flash flooding across the northern portion of the park. According to local law enforcement, flash flooding was reported at 1325PST in the north central portion of the park, around the popular Queen Valley Mountain bike area near the Sheep Pass group camp along highway 12. This area is east of Ryan Mountain. A Flash Flood Warning had been issued shortly before the flash flooding was reported. Fortunately no injuries were reported due to the flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and wet thunderstorms caused some local flash flooding in Siskiyou county on the evening of 08/04/2015.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a Mud/Dirt/Rock slide across California Highway 3 at Forest Mountain Summit between Yreka and Fort Jones.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed near the burn scar of the Powerhouse Fire. Heavy rain from the thunderstorms generated significant mud and debris flows across Lake Hughes Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms produced flash flooding in the mountains of Ventura County. The Ventura County Sheriff reported flash flooding at the intersection of Lockwood Valley Road and Boy Scout Camp road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lingering subtropical moisture produced strong thunderstorms and flash flooding across the mountains of Southern California. Reports of significant mud/debris flows as well as flash flooding were reported by law enforcement.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms produced flash flooding and mud/debris flows across the Antelope Valley. California Highway Patrol reported flash flooding along Largo Vista Road. Two vehicles were trapped by 2 to 4 feet of mud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm over Modoc County brought strong winds to the area on the afternoon of 7/7/15.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Juniper Creek RAWS recorded a gust to 59 mph sometime during this hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: July rounded out with another shot of monsoon moisture and thunderstorms as high pressure shifted back westward over the Four Corners on the 29th and 30th. The main drivers for storms were terrain and outflow boundary collisions. Scattered thunderstorms occurred mainly over the mountains and deserts with wide-ranging rainfall totals from a few tenths of an inch to locally over 2 inches. Flash flooding occurred in Idyllwild, Timoteo Canyon, Calimesa/Moreno Valley, along with water flowing in the Fish and Vallecito Creeks in Anza Borrego State Park. A few storms went severe, including quarter-sized hail and wind damage in Calimesa and wind damage from a microburst in Palm Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CHP reported a lightning strike that started a small fire near Interstate 8 and Kitchen Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical moisture left over from former Hurricane Dolores brought thunderstorms with hail and heavy rain into Northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tuolumne Public Works reported a culvert under Evergreen road at the Peach Growers Campground that got overwhelmed by the intensity of rainfall from a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continues in full force during the month of August, 2015. Temperatures continued to average above normal. Neither, Fresno or Bakersfield received any precipitation during the month of August.||The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The general trend of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures has continued this year. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, a decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the Southern Sierra Nevada, reduction in air quality due to persistent stagnant air, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. Also, communities in Central California interior face state mandated reductions in municipal water use.

The Willow Fire began on July 25, 2015 and continued to burn for the remainder of July and into August. It was human caused and was located 2 miles west of Wishon, CA. It burned 3383 acres as of July 31.

The Advance Fire was started by lightning on July 30, 2015 and was contained on August 2, 2015. It was located 5 miles north of Three Rivers, CA and burned 86 acres. The cost of containment is unknown.

Scattered strong thunderstorms developed across portions of eastern Riverside County during the evening hours on July 30th. The stronger storms produced locally heavy rain, with  peak rain rates between one and two inches per hour. The heavy rain led to small stream flooding and flash flooding in the area around Desert Center. Significant rainfall fell over portions of Interstate 10 east of Desert Center, and heavy rain led to flash flooding along State Route 177 near Desert Center. A Flash Flood Warning was issued for eastern Riverside County from 1738PST until 2030PST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of eastern Riverside county during the evening hours on July 30th, and the stronger storms produced very heavy rainfall which affected the area around Desert Center. Peak rainfall rates approached 2 inches per hour, and this led to some flooding and flash flooding of washes and roads around Desert Center. Significant amounts of rain fell on Interstate 10 east of Desert Center, and local law enforcement reported flooding over the roads near State Route 177 and Oasis Road at mile marker 2.40. This was approximately 2 miles northeast of the town of Desert Center. No accidents or injuries were reported due to the flash flooding. Law enforcement reported the flash flooding at about 2000PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continues in full force during the month of July, 2015. Temperatures continued to average above normal. July was a good month for precipitation as there were multiple monsoonal and tropical influxes of moisture into the area. However, this wasn't nearly enough to impact the drought situation. Rainfall totals for Fresno received 0.43 of an inch of rain, while Bakersfield received 0.04 inches. ||The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The 2013-2014 water year (July 1 - June 30) concluded with Fresno setting its second driest on record (4.81 inches) and Bakersfield setting its third driest (2.41 inches). For the calendar year, 2014 was the warmest year on record for Fresno and Bakersfield. The general trend of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures has continued this year. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, a decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the Southern Sierra Nevada, reduction in air quality due to persistent stagnant air, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. Also, communities in Central California interior face state mandated reductions in municipal water use.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed through the area resulting breezy to gusty winds through the mountain passes. Behind the front a strong low and mid level jet develop, which resulted in some additional gusty winds across the I-5 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet site at Laural Mountain reported a 59 MPH gust at 1026 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Cutca fire began at approximately 1500 PST on the 24th. The fire burned in steep and inaccessible terrain east of Cutca Valley and south of Long Canyon 2 miles north of the Palomar Observatory in the Cleveland National Forest. The fire burned a total of 150 before being fully contained on the 29th. The cause of the fire is unknown. No structures were threatened, no injuries, and no evacuations ordered.

A slow moving cold front moved through the forecast area during the morning hours, resulting in some shower activity. Behind the cold front snow levels dropped and fairly widespread rainfall was occurring in the Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported blowing dust along Highway 99 in the south valley between Bear Mountain Blvd and Houghton Road. 18 vehicles crashed and 15 people were injured. This resulted in closure of the south bound lanes of Highway 99.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front moved through the forecast area during the morning hours, resulting in some shower activity. Behind the cold front snow levels dropped and fairly widespread rainfall was occurring in the Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a mudslide across the Caliente Bodfish Road at Caliente Creek. Roadway was reported as impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Plenty of low level moisture and stable conditions under high pressure allowed dense fog to form each night and morning from January 11 to 16, 2015. In the days prior to this, there had been patchy fog, but it became more widespread throughout the San Joaquin Valley after a weak weather disturbance brought rain across Kern county on the January 10-11. ||The dense fog formed in Kings and Tulare counties in the evening of January 11, then spread into Fresno, Madera, and Merced counties as well as south into Kern county by early morning on January 12. Visibilities dropped to less than 500 feet in many areas. The fog lifted in most areas by noon on January 12. Dense fog also developed on parts of the Kern county desert areas near Edwards AFB and Ridgecrest. ||The fog quickly redeveloped in the early evening of January 12 and continue through the morning of January 13 with widespread visibilities below 1/4 mile and many areas reporting visibility below 800 feet. The fog cleared between 10 AM and noon. ||The worst of the fog occurred on January 14, with dense fog developing around midnight, but not clearing until nearly 2 pm. There were numerous school bus delays due to the dense fog and California Highway Patrol was pacing traffic on area highways. A major traffic accident occurred in Visalia involving three vehicles and resulting in major injuries to two people. ||The dense fog redeveloped on the evening of January 14 and continue through noon on January 15. It then quickly redeveloped early evening on the 15th, but lifted above 1/4 mile by 3 am on the 16th as a weak disturbance moving by to the north helped to lift the fog into a stratus deck.

A strong winter storm finally impacted California following up on nearly a month and a half of no rain and the driest January on record. The storm brought heavy rain, gusty winds, and damage to trees and power lines along with some minor flooding of urban areas. Rainfall amounts were heaviest in the mountains with 5-10 inches or more occurring. Generally 1-3.5 inches fell in low elevation areas and urban spots, with less than an inch in Southern Monterey County. Winds gusted 50-70 MPH with the highest gusts in the mountains of the region. Several small mudslides were reported with the largest occurring in the Santa Cruz Mountains north of Boulder Creek on Highway 9.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fuel tanker crashed in heavy rain on Highway 1 North of Jenner in Sonoma County. The tanker leaked about 9,000 gallons of diesel into the ocean.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed over the lower deserts of Imperial and eastern Riverside county during the afternoon and evening hours on July 19th. Due to an infusion of tropical moisture from former hurricane Dolores, some of the storms produced torrential rainfall with peak rainfall rates in excess of 3 inches per hour. The heavy rain led to episodes of flash flooding, one of which affected northeastern Imperial county during the evening hours. Flash flooding washed out highway 78 to the southwest of Cibola, and as a result the road was closed. Fortunately, no injuries were reported due to the flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms continued to develop across portions of eastern Imperial county during the evening hours on July 19th. Due to an infusion of tropical moisture from former hurricane Dolores, some of the storms produced torrential rainfall which led to localized flash flooding. Peak rain rates were well in excess of 3 inches per hour. At 1843PST, the California Highway Patrol reported that a portion of highway 78 to the southwest of Cibola was washed out due to flash flooding and as a result the highway was closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moslty clear skies and a cool air mass allowed temperatures to fall below freezing in the Shasta Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures ranged from 24 to 29 degrees.

Visibilities dropped to less than 500 feet in many areas. The fog lifted in most areas by noon on January 12. An upper trough moved through California on January 22, scouring out the low stratus deck that had been over the San Joaquin Valley for several days. However, the trough was not strong enough to completely scour out the low level moisture. A ridge of high pressure over the eastern Pacific began to build into northern California creating stable conditions with good radiational cooling. This led to areas of dense fog developing just after midnight on January 23. Visibility improved above 1 mile as the fog lifted into a low stratus deck by noon and remained through the remainder of the day. ||Dense fog redeveloped by early morning of January 24. The fog was particularly bad in Kern County where the California Highway Patrol reported areas of visibility less than 200 feet with pacing on many parts of Highway 99 and Highway 58 through Bakersfield. This time, the dense fog was the worst on the west side of the San Joaquin Valley with visibility of 200 to 500 feet along parts of Interstate 5. The weather pattern began to change on January 26, allowing the fog to clear by mid morning as a low pressure system off the northern Baja coast began spreading clouds across central California and spreading some rain as far north as Kern county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across southern Imperial county during the late morning and afternoon hours on July 18th. Due to an infusion of tropical moisture from former hurricane Dolores, some of the storms became very strong and capable of producing large hail in addition to torrential rainfall. One thunderstorm in particular near the town of Heber in far southern Imperial county produced large hail with diameters in excess of one inch. No damage was reported due to the very large hail, however.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated intense thunderstorms developed across far southern Imperial county during the late morning and early afternoon hours on July 18th. Partly due to an infusion of tropical moisture from former hurricane Dolores, the storms were capable of producing very large hail in addition to torrential rainfall. According to a local newspaper report, hailstones with a diameter of 1.25 inches were observed in the town of Heber, just to the south of El Centro. No damage was reported due to the large hail, however.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of eastern Riverside county during the late morning hours on July 18th and they continued into the afternoon. Due to an infusion of tropical moisture from former hurricane Dolores, some of the storms produced torrential rainfall which led to episodes of flash flooding. One area hit hard by the flash flooding was Desert Center and nearby roads such as Kaiser Road which runs up to Eagle Mountain, and highway 177. Flash flood warnings were issued for the area around Desert Center; fortunately there were no injuries associated with the flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the late morning hours on August 18th, scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of eastern Riverside county and they affected the area around the community of Desert Center. Due to an infusion of tropical moisture from former hurricane, some of the storms produced torrential rainfall with peak rain rates well in excess of 2 inches per hour. The heavy rain produced flash flooding around Desert Center during the early afternoon hours. According to a trained weather spotter, at 1135PST, water was reported to be flowing down both highway 177 and Kaiser Roads about 5 miles northeast of Desert Center. At least 3 inches of water was flowing down the roads. No accidents or injuries were reported due to the swiftly flowing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Round Fire began in far northern Inyo County and spread northwest into Mono County on strong southerly winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Round Fire began in the far northern Owens Valley north of Bishop and west of Highway 395. Strong southerly winds spread the fire generally north and west into Mono County. Only one structure, a hay shed, was destroyed in Inyo County. Much more damage occurred in Mono County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a wet December for most of the area, January returned to an extremely dry pattern with the California drought continuing in full force. This was the driest January on record for the state, with many locations receiving little or no precipitation for what is on average the wettest month of the year. Snow pack in the mountains was extremely low, about 25% of normal. |Extreme drought was detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor across the region, with exceptional drought for the northern San Joaquin Valley, the central and southern Sacramento Valley, the Burney Basin and the Sierra south of Interstate 80.  |Governor Jerry Brown declared a drought emergency for the entire state of California January 17, 2014 and this continued to be in effect. |Impacts to the region for January included strong conservation efforts within most communities, a significant decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the Northern Sierra Nevada, reduction in air quality due to persistent stagnant air, and a reduction of ground water resources which are replacing diminished reservoir water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant drought continues to impact to the region for January, including a large reduction in snow related tourism in the Northern Sierra Nevada. Multiple downhill and cross country ski resorts were forced to close due to a lack of snow. Resorts which remained open were forced to produce artificial snow, and scale back the number of runs open. Other winter activities such as snowmobiling and snow shoeing were also greatly affected by the lack of snow pack. Restaurants and hotels in the area which rely on winter tourism suffered a significant drop in business.||Many farmers are already planning to scale back crop planting acreage due to decreased irrigation water allocations again in 2015.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light winds and clear skies allowed dense morning fog to develop for several days. Numerous car accidents occurred, including two multiple car crashes with fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 2 vehicle car crash occurred on Highway 4 off Van Allen Rd., east of the city of Stockton, resulting in 5 fatalities and 3 injuries. The accident occurred at approximately 6:30 am PST and is thought to be related to poor visibility from fog. First responders reported visibility less than 100 feet when they arrived on scene. Visibility at Stockton Municipal Airport, which is about 10 miles west, was measured at a quarter mile at 6 and 7 am PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continued in full force during the month of January, 2015. Precipitation fell on only a few days of the month, and mostly across Kern County. Snowfall in the Sierra Nevada was limited to the highest elevations due to the warm nature of the storms that did move through. Temperatures remained well above normal throughout central California.  ||The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The 2013-2014 water year (July 1 - June 30) concluded with Fresno setting its second driest on record (4.81 inches) and Bakersfield setting its third driest (2.41 inches). For the calendar year, 2014 was the warmest year on record for Fresno and Bakersfield. The trend of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures continued into 2015. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, a decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the Southern Sierra Nevada, reduction in air quality due to persistent stagnant air, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains.

However, the trough was not strong enough to completely scour out the low level moisture. A ridge of high pressure over the eastern Pacific began to build into northern California creating stable conditions with good radiational cooling. This led to areas of dense fog developing just after midnight on January 23. Visibility improved above 1 mile as the fog lifted into a low stratus deck by noon and remained through the remainder of the day. ||Dense fog redeveloped by early morning of January 24. The fog was particularly bad in Kern County where the California Highway Patrol reported areas of visibility less than 200 feet with pacing on many parts of Highway 99 and Highway 58 through Bakersfield. The fog once again lifted into a low stratus deck by afternoon. Then dense fog developed again late in the evening and persisted through noon on January 25. The fog lifted into a low stratus deck which persisted through the afternoon before clearing in the evening. This allowed areas of dense fog to redevelop in the early morning hours of January 26. This time, the dense fog was the worst on the west side of the San Joaquin Valley with visibility of 200 to 500 feet along parts of Interstate 5. The weather pattern began to change on January 26, allowing the fog to clear by mid morning as a low pressure system off the northern Baja coast began spreading clouds across central California and spreading some rain as far north as Kern county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas of visibility less than 1/4 mile near Hanford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm finally impacted California following up on nearly a month and a half of no rain and the driest January on record. The storm brought heavy rain, gusty winds, and damage to trees and power lines along with some minor flooding of urban areas. Rainfall amounts were heaviest in the mountains with 5-10 inches or more occurring. Generally 1-3.5 inches fell in low elevation areas and urban spots, with less than an inch in Southern Monterey County. Winds gusted 50-70 MPH with the highest gusts in the mountains of the region. Several small mudslides were reported with the largest occurring in the Santa Cruz Mountains north of Boulder Creek on Highway 9.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 72 Hour Rainfall Total of 4.80 inches was measured at elevation 2339 Feet on the Middle Peak RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early-season monsoon pattern set up across the southwest U.S., bringing several days of enhanced mid-level moisture from the southeast and enough instability for scattered showers and thunderstorms for the last few days of the month. Scattered thunderstorms developed over the high desert east of the forecast area on the 28th and drifted west, producing lightning and some light rainfall amounts under one-quarter inch (Green Valley Lake won at 0.3 inches) over the San Jacinto Mountains and portions of southwest San Bernardino County, altogether a fairly small coverage area. The 29th saw thunderstorms trying to form over the southern San Diego County Mountains, but quickly dissipating without much effect as they drifted off the mountain crests. A disturbance moving through Baja on the 30th produced an active day of thunderstorms over southern California, reaching all the way to the coast with prolific lightning and rainfall for several areas. Lightning sparked a few small fires near Poway and La Mesa. A few reports of roadway flooding and mudslides came in from the Poway and Ramona areas that day. Amounts were mostly under one-tenth of an inch along the coast, one-tenth to one-half inch in the valleys, and up to three-quarters of an inch in the San Diego County mountains. Other mountain areas were largely missed by this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CHP reported lightning striking a wooden power pole with the pole catching on fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continued in full force during the month of January, 2015. Precipitation fell on only a few days of the month, and mostly across Kern County. Snowfall in the Sierra Nevada was limited to the highest elevations due to the warm nature of the storms that did move through. Temperatures remained well above normal throughout central California.  ||The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The 2013-2014 water year (July 1 - June 30) concluded with Fresno setting its second driest on record (4.81 inches) and Bakersfield setting its third driest (2.41 inches). For the calendar year, 2014 was the warmest year on record for Fresno and Bakersfield. The trend of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures continued into 2015. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, a decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the Southern Sierra Nevada, reduction in air quality due to persistent stagnant air, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Crescent City ASOS reported a gust to 64 mph.  Law enforcement reported a large limb of a Redwood tree fell on a Humboldt State University residence hall.  This caused damage to the third floor of the building.  They also reported numerous trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface high pressure area over the Great Basin resulted in gusty east to northeast winds near the mountain crests, in the coastal foothills, and western inland valleys, including the Inland Empire. Gusty winds began on the 21st, reaching peak strength on the 23rd through 24th. Several stations recorded gusts above 65 mph. Damage included several trees and power lines down in the Inland Empire and foothills of San Diego County. There were also reports of two semi trucks overturned, one in Rancho Cucamonga, and the other just west of Pine Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred at the Alpine RAWS site between 0152 to 2352 PST on the 24th. During this time, sustained winds out of the NE reached 35-45 mph with gusts in the high 50s to low 70s. A peak gust of 72 mph was recorded between 0952 and 1052 PST on the 24th. A wind gust of 73 mph was also measured an 0921 PST near the city of Poway by a trained weather spotter using a vortex anemometer. An additional wind gust of 62 mph was recorded at the Hellhole Canyon Mesonet station between 0610 and 0620 PST. Finally, California Highway Patrol reported power lines and a pole down across both lanes on Sutherland Dam Road at 1328 PST.

The California Highway Patrol also reported a semi truck knocked on its side near the intersection of Interstate 8 and State Highway 79 due to strong winds at 0937 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought precipitation to central California on January 27, 2015, and was followed by an area of high pressure and stable conditions which led to dense fog development in the San Joaquin Valley each late night and morning on January 28-31. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper level low pressure system drifted southward across the area, bringing widespread thunderstorm activity to Southwestern California. The thunderstorms generated heavy rainfall which resulted in mud and debris flows across the Pacific Coast Highway. Additionally, there were many reports of small hail (pea size) across the area in association with the thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm produced heavy rain over the southern part of the Springs Fire burn scar. This heavy rain generated some flash flooding and mud flows across the Pacific Coast Highway between Las Posas Road and Deer Path Road. Both sides of the Pacific Coast Highway were closed due to the mudslide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moved through California on January 22, scouring out the low stratus deck that had been over the San Joaquin Valley for several days. However, the trough was not strong enough to completely scour out the low level moisture. A ridge of high pressure over the eastern Pacific began to build into northern California creating stable conditions with good radiational cooling. This led to areas of dense fog developing just after midnight on January 23. Visibility improved above 1 mile as the fog lifted into a low stratus deck by noon and remained through the remainder of the day. ||Dense fog redeveloped by early morning of January 24. The fog was particularly bad in Kern County where the California Highway Patrol reported areas of visibility less than 200 feet with pacing on many parts of Highway 99 and Highway 58 through Bakersfield. The fog once again lifted into a low stratus deck by afternoon. Then dense fog developed again late in the evening and persisted through noon on January 25. The fog lifted into a low stratus deck which persisted through the afternoon before clearing in the evening. This allowed areas of dense fog to redevelop in the early morning hours of January 26. This time, the dense fog was the worst on the west side of the San Joaquin Valley with visibility of 200 to 500 feet along parts of Interstate 5. The weather pattern began to change on January 26, allowing the fog to clear by mid morning as a low pressure system off the northern Baja coast began spreading clouds across central California and spreading some rain as far north as Kern county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas visibility less than 1/4 mile, including 200-500 ft visibility along Interstate 5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continued in full force during the month of January, 2015. Precipitation fell on only a few days of the month, and mostly across Kern County. Snowfall in the Sierra Nevada was limited to the highest elevations due to the warm nature of the storms that did move through. Temperatures remained well above normal throughout central California.  ||The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The 2013-2014 water year (July 1 - June 30) concluded with Fresno setting its second driest on record (4.81 inches) and Bakersfield setting its third driest (2.41 inches). For the calendar year, 2014 was the warmest year on record for Fresno and Bakersfield. The trend of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures continued into 2015. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, a decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the Southern Sierra Nevada, reduction in air quality due to persistent stagnant air, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface high pressure area over the Great Basin resulted in gusty east to northeast winds near the mountain crests, in the coastal foothills, and western inland valleys, including the Inland Empire. Gusty winds began on the 21st, reaching peak strength on the 23rd through 24th. Several stations recorded gusts above 65 mph. Damage included several trees and power lines down in the Inland Empire and foothills of San Diego County. There were also reports of two semi trucks overturned, one in Rancho Cucamonga, and the other just west of Pine Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred at the Highland Springs RAWS between 0516-0716 PST on the 24th. During this time, winds were sustained out of the ENE at 40-50 mph with gusts in the 60s. A peak gust of 69 mph was recorded between 0616-0716 PST. The Ontario Airport ASOS recorded a peak gust of 64 mph around 0405 PST on the 24th. ABC7 news reported several trees down due to wind in Rancho Cucamonga and Alta Loma, as well as power lines down and customers without electricity in Fontana. One large tree fell on a car, completely crushing it, in Rancho Cucamonga. CHP also reported a big rig flipped over into a ditch along Highway 210 near the Interstate 15 junction, likely wind-related.

A strong winter storm finally impacted California following up on nearly a month and a half of no rain and the driest January on record. The storm brought heavy rain, gusty winds, and damage to trees and power lines along with some minor flooding of urban areas. Rainfall amounts were heaviest in the mountains with 5-10 inches or more occurring. Generally 1-3.5 inches fell in low elevation areas and urban spots, with less than an inch in Southern Monterey County. Winds gusted 50-70 MPH with the highest gusts in the mountains of the region. Several small mudslides were reported with the largest occurring in the Santa Cruz Mountains north of Boulder Creek on Highway 9.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree blown down in the roadway on the 300 block of Marshall Drive and Lakewood Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought precipitation to central California on January 27, 2015, and was followed by an area of high pressure and stable conditions which led to dense fog development in the San Joaquin Valley each late night and morning on January 28-31. Widespread visibility below 1/4 mile occurred each day, with the visibility dropping below 500 feet in some areas. The dense fog led to airport delays in Fresno, numerous school bus delays each day, and numerous traffic accidents, two of which resulted in fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas visibility 500-700 feet. A multi-vehicle accident involving 2 big rigs, an empty fuel truck and a pickup truck occurred along Highway 41 around 3 AM. There were 2 injuries and 1 fatality.

Cooling temperatures overnight after light rain the previous evening led to isolated dense fog over the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two spotters reported visibility of 1/4 mile or less along Highway 62 west of Twentynine Palms and also in the Yucca Valley/Yucca Mesa area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cooling temperatures overnight after light rain the previous evening led to isolated dense fog over the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain, low clouds, and fog led to four car crashes in the Morongo Basin with two (indirect) injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moved through California on January 22, scouring out the low stratus deck that had been over the San Joaquin Valley for several days. However, the trough was not strong enough to completely scour out the low level moisture. A ridge of high pressure over the eastern Pacific began to build into northern California creating stable conditions with good radiational cooling. This led to areas of dense fog developing just after midnight on January 23. Visibility improved above 1 mile as the fog lifted into a low stratus deck by noon and remained through the remainder of the day. ||Dense fog redeveloped by early morning of January 24. The fog was particularly bad in Kern County where the California Highway Patrol reported areas of visibility less than 200 feet with pacing on many parts of Highway 99 and Highway 58 through Bakersfield. The fog once again lifted into a low stratus deck by afternoon. Then dense fog developed again late in the evening and persisted through noon on January 25. The fog lifted into a low stratus deck which persisted through the afternoon before clearing in the evening. This allowed areas of dense fog to redevelop in the early morning hours of January 26. This time, the dense fog was the worst on the west side of the San Joaquin Valley with visibility of 200 to 500 feet along parts of Interstate 5. The weather pattern began to change on January 26, allowing the fog to clear by mid morning as a low pressure system off the northern Baja coast began spreading clouds across central California and spreading some rain as far north as Kern county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread visibility less than 1/4 mile. CHP pacing on parts of Highway 99.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Plenty of low level moisture and stable conditions under high pressure allowed dense fog to form each night and morning from January 11 to 16, 2015. In the days prior to this, there had been patchy fog, but it became more widespread throughout the San Joaquin Valley after a weak weather disturbance brought rain across Kern county on the January 10-11. ||The dense fog formed in Kings and Tulare counties in the evening of January 11, then spread into Fresno, Madera, and Merced counties as well as south into Kern county by early morning on January 12. Visibilities dropped to less than 500 feet in many areas. The fog lifted in most areas by noon on January 12. Dense fog also developed on parts of the Kern county desert areas near Edwards AFB and Ridgecrest. ||The fog quickly redeveloped in the early evening of January 12 and continue through the morning of January 13 with widespread visibilities below 1/4 mile and many areas reporting visibility below 800 feet. The fog cleared between 10 AM and noon. ||The worst of the fog occurred on January 14, with dense fog developing around midnight, but not clearing until nearly 2 pm. There were numerous school bus delays due to the dense fog and California Highway Patrol was pacing traffic on area highways. A major traffic accident occurred in Visalia involving three vehicles and resulting in major injuries to two people. ||The dense fog redeveloped on the evening of January 14 and continue through noon on January 15. It then quickly redeveloped early evening on the 15th, but lifted above 1/4 mile by 3 am on the 16th as a weak disturbance moving by to the north helped to lift the fog into a stratus deck.

A strong winter storm finally impacted California following up on nearly a month and a half of no rain and the driest January on record. The storm brought heavy rain, gusty winds, and damage to trees and power lines along with some minor flooding of urban areas. Rainfall amounts were heaviest in the mountains with 5-10 inches or more occurring. Generally 1-3.5 inches fell in low elevation areas and urban spots, with less than an inch in Southern Monterey County. Winds gusted 50-70 MPH with the highest gusts in the mountains of the region. Several small mudslides were reported with the largest occurring in the Santa Cruz Mountains north of Boulder Creek on Highway 9.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree branch was blown down into the slow lane of Eastbound Interstate 80 at Hill Top.
