EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought heavy rain across the areas, with local flooding.  A brief period of strong winds were reported, gusting to 50 to 80 miles an hour over the mountains of eastern Shasta and Plumas counties. These winds brought down trees, causing damage to homes, vehicles, and creating power outages. In Plumas county, approximately 67 buildings suffered wind damage; State Strike Teams of Hand Crews were tasked to assist in emergency debris/tree removal. Three schools and six public buildings were damaged as well. Major tree damage was reported in western Lassen County into Lassen National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 7.77 inches of rain measured over 3 days, with a trace of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough developed over northern California and deepened along the West Coast with an overland trajectory, eventually moving inland through southern California. The system contained enough moisture, aided by orographic lift, to result in moderate to locally heavy rainfall over and west of the mountains, as well as significant snowfall above 5500 feet. Locally gusty west winds also occurred along the deserts slopes and thorough the mountain passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trained spotters and a CoCoRaHS observer reported 12 hour snowfall totals of 3 to 6 inches in the city of Big Bear as of 0900PST on the 23rd. Elsewhere in the San Bernardino Mountains spotters reported lighter 12 hour totals at 0900PST on the 23rd, with 1 to 4 inches reported by trained spotters in Wrightwood, 2 inches reported by a trained spotter in Running Springs, and 1 inch reported by a CoCoRaHS observer in Fawnskin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an extremely dry January for most areas, February had below normal precipitation, with a few exceptions. Extreme drought was detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor across most the region, with exceptional drought for the northern San Joaquin Valley, the central and southern Sacramento Valley, the Burney Basin and the Sierra south of Interstate 80. Some temporary improvement to Severe drought was seen over the Coastal Range due to a wet system early in the month.|Governor Jerry Brown declared a drought emergency for the entire state of California January 17, 2014 and this continued to be in effect.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Impacts to the region for February included strong conservation efforts within most communities, a decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the Northern Sierra Nevada, reduction in air quality due to persistent stagnant air, and a reduction of ground water resources which are replacing diminished reservoir water. Farmers planned to reduce planting acreage due to projected reduction in irrigation water allocations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of heavy snow caused travel problems in the Sierra. The heaviest snow was mainly over Interstate 80, where heavy convective snow showers occurred. Snow amounts of 4 to 10 inches were measured above 4500 feet. |Thunderstorms locally brought large amounts of small hail, covering roads in Glenn and Nevada counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant traffic delays occurred on Interstate 80 when a period of heavy snow caused several big rigs to slide and block Donner Pass, with long traffic backups resulting. Many cars were ill prepared for winter driving conditions and multiple car accidents were reported. CHP reported that 1000 cars needed to be turned around due to no chains or studded tires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of dry troughs of low pressure moved across California in the last few days of the March. The trough that moved through on March 31, increased the surface pressure gradients resulting in strong, gusty winds across the mountains and desert areas of Kern County, along with breezy conditions in the San Joaquin Valley. Winds were west-northwesterly 15-30 mph with gusts generally 45-55 mph across the mountains and desert areas. However, there were some gusts of 58-66 mph in a few wind-prone locations. The gusty winds continued into April 1st and 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Jawbone RAWS station reported sustained winds of 40-46 mph with gusts to 61 mph. These winds continued into the morning of April 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air that moved into the San Joaquin Valley at the end of December continued to bring below freezing temperatures on the 1st of January. Kern, Tulare, Kings, Fresno, Madera and Merced counties all experienced temperatures dropping below 32 degrees with many areas dropping to 26-28 degrees in areas without protection for and hour or two. A few locations had minimum temperatures of 24-25 degrees for a brief period. According to Citrus Mutual, utilization of wind machines and water raised grove temperatures on average by three to four degrees thereby assuring no significant fruit damage for any of the major varieties, navel oranges, lemons and mandarins.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread temperatures below 32 degrees for several hours. Temperatures dropped to a minimum of 24-25 degrees. No crop damage reported.

According to the Mammoth Times, power lines were downed which caused widespread power outages north of Mammoth for at least a day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elevated long period swell brought high surf to the beaches of Southwest California from the 8th to the 10th of February. There was 1 fatality reported near Laguna Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the 8th at 1225PST surf of 5 to 8 ft was reported by Surfline at Blacks Beach. Surf fell into the 4 to 6 ft range on the 9th. Increases in surf occurred on the 10th with  Surfline reporting 5 to 8 ft surf at Oceanside, Pacific, Ocean, Imperial, and Salt Creek Beaches at 0700PST. Also on the 10th, a spotter reported 8 to 10 ft surf at Sunset Cliffs Beach and lifeguards reported 7 ft surf at Moonlight Beach, both at 0800PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front pushed into the West on the 6th, bringing widespread high winds along with areas of enhanced downslope winds in the lee of the Sierra.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme downslope winds in the lee of the Sierra prompted rapid spread of the Round Fire, which raced up a drainage and burned 40 homes in Swall Meadows.  The size of the fire was 6,537 acres according to CalOES. Precipitation arrived early in the morning on the 7th which dramatically slowed the fire, although the fire was not completely contained until the 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of disturbances moved into Southern California on January 10 and 11, 2015, and brushed across Kern county and the Tulare county portion of the Sierra Nevada. Only light precipitation occurred on January 10, but the system that moved through on the 11th was stronger and produced locally heavy rain. This resulted in some street flooding in the Bakersfield area along with some rock slides along Highway 178 in the Kern county mountains, and along Redrock-Randsburg Road in the Kern county desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts of 0.5 to 1 inch in 24 hours over the Kern county produced ponding of water/flooding on roadways in the south end of the San Joaquin Valley, in and south  of Bakersfield. The flooded areas included part of Highway 99 between Rosedale Highway and Olive Drive in Bakersfield and Highway 119 near Buena Vista.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elevated long period swell brought high surf to the beaches of Southwest California from the 8th to the 10th of February. There was 1 fatality reported near Laguna Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1330PST on the 8th Laguna beach lifeguards reported an 18 year old male swept off the rocks and into a cave by 5 to 8 ft surf. Lifeguards were unable to locate the 18 year old, and he became a confirmed fatality on the 19th of February when his body was recovered near the south end of Shell Beach in Three Arch Bay. Surf of 8 to 10 ft with occasional sets to 15 ft was reported by the Huntington Beach lifeguards from 1300PST on the 8th until 1600PST on the 10th. At 0700PST on the 10th Surfline reported 5 to 7 ft surf at Salt Creek Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough developed over northern California and deepened along the West Coast with an overland trajectory, eventually moving inland through southern California. The system contained enough moisture, aided by orographic lift, to result in moderate to locally heavy rainfall over and west of the mountains, as well as significant snowfall above 5500 feet. Locally gusty west winds also occurred along the deserts slopes and thorough the mountain passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ski resorts of Mountain High and Snow Valley reported 10 to 14 inches of snow above 7000 feet in the 12 hours preceding 0900 PST on the 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely dry March followed a below normal February for most areas. This continued the 4th consecutive year of drought for the region. Mountain snowfall was very limited for the month. This along with record warmth over the area resulted in the lowest snow pack levels on record for the time of year. By the end of March, the snow pack was only about 5 percent of normal levels. Melting snow pack supplies about a third of the annual water supply for California. Reservoirs across the area by the end of March were already well below normal levels.||Extreme drought was detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor across most the region, with exceptional drought for the northern San Joaquin Valley, the central and southern Sacramento Valley, the Burney Basin and the Sierra south of Interstate 80.| |Governor Jerry Brown declared a drought emergency for the entire state of California January 17, 2014 and this continued to be in effect.  ||Local Emergency Proclamations have been issued for El Dorado, Glenn, Lake, Plumas,  San Joaquin, Shasta, Sutter,  Tuolumne, and Yuba counties. The cities of Live Oak Lodi, and Ripon continue in a drought emergency. Drought task forces have been established in Butte, Lake, Nevada, Placer, Plumas, Sacramento, San Joaquin, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tehama, Tuolumne, and Yolo counties to coordinate response to the drought.||Voluntary water conservation efforts reduced water usage by 3.6 percent statewide compared to March 2014. Governor Brown ordered mandatory restrictions of 25 percent statewide, the first time such mandatory restrictions have been enacted.||Agriculture has been heavily impacted, with numerous farm and farm related jobs lost. Spring planting is being scaled back significantly, with many existing tree crops and vines being removed due to a lack of irrigation water. The state continued to supply food assistance to those impacted. California Disaster Assistance Act money continued to be provided for those who have lost drinking water due to dry wells.||There was a large decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the mountains due to the lack of snow, with many resorts closing early.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Impacts to the region for March included conservation efforts within most foothill communities. Increased water conservation measures were passed by the state to increase reductions further by limiting water use for lawn watering and added incentives to limit water use in hotels and restaurants  There was a continued reduction of ground water resources which are being used to replace diminished reservoir water. Agricultural impacts included orchard reduction, limits on planting and increased costs due to reliance on groundwater. Sutter County alone has lost an estimated 1100 agriculture related jobs. The Lake Don Pedro Community (La Grange and Coulterville)  adopted mandatory water conservation measures and water use restrictions effective March 9th due to the drought emergency and severe lack of water supply in Lake McClure, which is expected to go dry this summer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowfall exceeding 8-14 inches caused travel problems in the mountains, with some light accumulations reported down to elevations around 2500 feet. Thunderstorms locally brought both large hail and large amounts of small hail, causing traffic accidents and spin outs. Multiple funnel clouds were reported, but there were no confirmed touchdowns. Heavy rainfall of 1 to 2 inches was measured, with some ponding on roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A possible funnel cloud was sited at Marconi Ave. and the Capital City Expressway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure moving through California brought strong winds to the Kern County Mountains and Desert areas on March 23. Although most of the wind gusts were 40 to 50 mph, there were a few gusts as high as 56 mph at Jawbone RAWS and Cache Creek APRS station. At the Bird Spring Pass RAWS station, winds gusted 70 to 79 mph for several hours, while Mojave AWOS gusted to 62 mph. No damage was reported from the winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NWS owned APRS station at Mojave reported sustained winds 40-48 mph with gusts to 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm produced high winds in the Owens Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust occurred at the Bishop Airport (KBIH).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the west coast brought stable conditions and areas of dense fog over the San Joaquin Valley on February 19-20, 2015. Dense fog began developing around midnight on February 19, becoming widespread by early morning particularly in from Merced to Madera and in the Hanford, Lemoore, and Visalia areas along Highway 198. Several school districts reported bus delays. Several injury traffic accidents were also reported. There was also one fatality as a car collided with a pole in Visalia. The dense fog lifted around 1030 PST. Areas of dense fog redeveloped after 2300 PST on February 19 and persisted through 0900 PST on February 20. The fog was particularly dense in the Fresno and Lemoore areas where visibility was less than 1/8 mile, as well as in Kern county along Highway 99 where visibility less than 200 feet resulted in CHP pacing traffic along Highway 99 through and just north of Bakersfield. By mid morning of February 20, the dense fog lifted into a low stratus deck which persisted over the San Joaquin Valley through day and into the Saturday, February 21, 2015.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several school districts had bus delays due to the dense fog.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread visibility less than 1/4 mile, including at the Merced and Madera ASOS. Several school districts had bus delays due to the dense fog. There were several traffic collisions in which reduced visibility was a factor. Two of these collisions resulted on minor injuries. One injury occurred in a collision involving two vehicles near Kingsburg. The other injury occurred near Selma where a vehicle overturned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent spring storm system brought strong winds and thunderstorms to much of the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak gust occurred at Bicycle Lake Airfield (KBYS).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent spring storm system brought strong winds and thunderstorms to much of the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 178 was closed south of Badwater Basin due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river event, with several embedded short waves, impacted much of California from Thursday 5 February 2015 through Monday 9 February 2015. Due to the subtropical nature of the storm, snow levels remained very high (8000'+) throughout the storm with only a few inches reported. Precipitation across the San Joaquin Valley ranged from a few hundredths near Bakersfield to around three-quarters of an inch near Merced. In the Sierra Nevada and foothills, rainfall amounts were generally 2 to 4 inches with the highest amount observed being 5.16 at Nature Point (west of Oakhurst). Fresno received a storm total rain amount of 0.47 and Bakersfield 0.07.||At 1130 PM on Friday 6 February 2015, a mudslide was reported by CHP east of Coarsegold resulting in a one-car accident (no injuries). Law enforcement also reported flooding on Highway 41 near Coarsegold at the same time.||This system also brought gusty winds to the west side of the San Joaquin Valley where gusts were generally 35 to 45 mph on February 6. These winds resulted in blowing dust that severely reduced visibility along Highway 41 in Kings County, closing part of the road for several hours. Wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph also occurred on February 8 into the early morning of February 9, 2015 with a few gusts to near 60 mph through the Pacheco Pass and at the Panoche Road RAWS.  Winds were also gusty across the Kern County mountains and over the high elevations of the Sierra in Tulare County with gusts 40 to 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusts of 45 to 60 mph recorded at Pacheco Pass.

CZ_NAME: MOUNTAINS SOUTHWEST SHASTA COUNTY TO NORTHERN LAKE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The long term drought continues as April was yet another below normal month for precipitation for much of the area. There was some mountain snowfall, but this did little to improve the snow pack, which remained at the lowest levels on record. By the end of April, the snow pack was only about 4 percent of normal levels. As a result, reservoirs across the area by the end of April remained well below normal levels with little or no spring rise, due to the lack of snow melt.||Extreme drought was detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor across most the region, with exceptional drought for the northern San Joaquin Valley, the central and southern Sacramento Valley, the Burney Basin and the Sierra south of Interstate 80.| |Governor Jerry Brown declared a drought emergency for the entire state of California January 17, 2014 and this continued to be in effect.  ||Local Emergency Proclamations have been issued for El Dorado, Glenn, Lake, Plumas,  San Joaquin, Shasta, Sutter,  Tuolumne, and Yuba counties. The cities of Live Oak Lodi, and Ripon continue in a drought emergency. Drought task forces have been established in Butte, Lake, Nevada, Placer, Plumas, Sacramento, San Joaquin, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tehama, Tuolumne, and Yolo counties to coordinate response to the drought.||Mandatory water reductions of 25 percent as ordered by Governor Brown were in effect statewide, the first time such mandatory restrictions have been enacted.||Agriculture has been heavily impacted, with numerous farm and farm related jobs lost. Spring planting is being scaled back significantly, with many existing tree crops and vines being removed due to a lack of irrigation water. The state continued to supply food assistance to those impacted. California Disaster Assistance Act money continued to be provided for those who have lost drinking water due to dry wells.||There was a large decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the mountains due to the lack of snow, with many resorts closing early in the month or having already closed in March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought is impacting Lake County, with farmers clipping some wine grape vines, reducing irrigation needed this summer, while reducing their harvest. Agriculture and related businesses have lost jobs.

The dense fog lifted around 1030 PST. Areas of dense fog redeveloped after 2300 PST on February 19 and persisted through 0900 PST on February 20. The fog was particularly dense in the Fresno and Lemoore areas where visibility was less than 1/8 mile, as well as in Kern county along Highway 99 where visibility less than 200 feet resulted in CHP pacing traffic along Highway 99 through and just north of Bakersfield. By mid morning of February 20, the dense fog lifted into a low stratus deck which persisted over the San Joaquin Valley through day and into the Saturday, February 21, 2015.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread visibility less than 1/4 mile in Kings county, including at the Hanford ASOS. Lemoore ASOS reported visibility of 1/16 mile. Several school districts had bus delays due to the dense fog. There was a fatality traffic accident when a Dodge truck collided with a pole in Visalia around 0400 PST on February 19.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent spring storm system brought strong winds and thunderstorms to much of the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 60 mph was recorded at Bicycle Lake ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper low off the Central California coast produced scattered showers and thunderstorms across the southern and eastern portions of the San Francisco Bay Area.  These showers and thunderstorms produced small hail. In addition, two funnel clouds were sighted in San Jose. The first was sighted at 9:20 am along highway 101 in San Jose. The second around 1:30 pm in East San Jose. Neither funnel cloud touched down and no damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: San Francisco news station forwarded two photos of a funnel cloud taken by two different members of the public in east San Jose during the early afternoon of May 14, 2015.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system dropping south along the California coast brought showers and thunderstorms to central California March 1st and 2nd. Although many areas received some rain, the greatest impacts from the storm were across Kern county. Parts of Kern county received 0.25 inches to 0.75 inches of rain, especially the area in the lower Kern River Canyon east of Bakersfield. This resulted in minor rock and mud slides onto Highway 178, closing the road for several hours. On the west side of Kern county, rain was a contributing factor the the development of a sink hole in the city of Taft.||Snow also fell across the mountains of Kern County during the early hours of March 2nd. Snowfall amounts ranged from 1 inch to 5.5 inches at elevations above 5000 feet. Since snow levels remained above the level of the major highways, no significant impacts were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 0.49 inches fell at the APRS station in Taft. The rain contributed to the development of a sinkhole that was 200 feet long, 1 to 2 feet wide, and 1 to 4 feet deep.  Five homes were evacuated as a precaution, but no damage was reported to any buildings or utilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The long term drought continues as April was yet another below normal month for precipitation for much of the area. There was some mountain snowfall, but this did little to improve the snow pack, which remained at the lowest levels on record. By the end of April, the snow pack was only about 4 percent of normal levels. As a result, reservoirs across the area by the end of April remained well below normal levels with little or no spring rise, due to the lack of snow melt.||Extreme drought was detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor across most the region, with exceptional drought for the northern San Joaquin Valley, the central and southern Sacramento Valley, the Burney Basin and the Sierra south of Interstate 80.| |Governor Jerry Brown declared a drought emergency for the entire state of California January 17, 2014 and this continued to be in effect.  ||Local Emergency Proclamations have been issued for El Dorado, Glenn, Lake, Plumas,  San Joaquin, Shasta, Sutter,  Tuolumne, and Yuba counties. The cities of Live Oak Lodi, and Ripon continue in a drought emergency. Drought task forces have been established in Butte, Lake, Nevada, Placer, Plumas, Sacramento, San Joaquin, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tehama, Tuolumne, and Yolo counties to coordinate response to the drought.||Mandatory water reductions of 25 percent as ordered by Governor Brown were in effect statewide, the first time such mandatory restrictions have been enacted.||Agriculture has been heavily impacted, with numerous farm and farm related jobs lost. Spring planting is being scaled back significantly, with many existing tree crops and vines being removed due to a lack of irrigation water. The state continued to supply food assistance to those impacted. California Disaster Assistance Act money continued to be provided for those who have lost drinking water due to dry wells.||There was a large decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the mountains due to the lack of snow, with many resorts closing early in the month or having already closed in March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought continued to impact the northern Sacramento Valley, with farmers reducing acres planted and removing some trees from orchards. Agriculture and related businesses have lost jobs. Air quality has suffered, with particulate levels at the highest levels in a decade for some locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the west coast brought stable conditions and areas of dense fog over the San Joaquin Valley on February 19-20, 2015. Dense fog began developing around midnight on February 19, becoming widespread by early morning particularly in from Merced to Madera and in the Hanford, Lemoore, and Visalia areas along Highway 198. Several school districts reported bus delays. Several injury traffic accidents were also reported. There was also one fatality as a car collided with a pole in Visalia. The dense fog lifted around 1030 PST. Areas of dense fog redeveloped after 2300 PST on February 19 and persisted through 0900 PST on February 20. The fog was particularly dense in the Fresno and Lemoore areas where visibility was less than 1/8 mile, as well as in Kern county along Highway 99 where visibility less than 200 feet resulted in CHP pacing traffic along Highway 99 through and just north of Bakersfield. By mid morning of February 20, the dense fog lifted into a low stratus deck which persisted over the San Joaquin Valley through day and into the Saturday, February 21, 2015.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across portions of eastern Riverside county during the afternoon hours on April 21st, and for the most part the storms were not especially strong. However, an isolated storm developed about 6 miles north of Desert Center, and the storm produced a small, rope-like tornado estimated to be EF0 in strength. Numerous photos were taken of this storm by the public, and fortunately the damage that it caused was not substantial. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across portions of far southeast California, including eastern Riverside county, during the afternoon hours on April 21st. For the most part the storms were not especially strong, however one thunderstorm located about 6 miles north of Desert Center produced a small, rope-like tornado which traveled nearly 10 miles over mostly open country. The tornado was first reported from an aircraft pilot at about 1447PST and local broadcast media later confirmed the tornado with photographs. Additionally, photos and videos from the public showed the tornado, which did not produce any injuries. The tornado did produce modest damage; several windshields were broken from flying rocks and a number of solar panels were destroyed. The tornado was officially confirmed through an NWS remote storm survey and was classified as an EF0 tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Northern and Central California during the early morning hours of Tuesday, April 7. The front generated local strong and gusty winds, brief heavy rain, and localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roadway flooding and debris caused multiple lane closures on highway 101 near the Red Barn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river event, with several embedded short waves, impacted much of California from Thursday 5 February 2015 through Monday 9 February 2015. Due to the subtropical nature of the storm, snow levels remained very high (8000'+) throughout the storm with only a few inches reported. Precipitation across the San Joaquin Valley ranged from a few hundredths near Bakersfield to around three-quarters of an inch near Merced. In the Sierra Nevada and foothills, rainfall amounts were generally 2 to 4 inches with the highest amount observed being 5.16 at Nature Point (west of Oakhurst). Fresno received a storm total rain amount of 0.47 and Bakersfield 0.07.||At 1130 PM on Friday 6 February 2015, a mudslide was reported by CHP east of Coarsegold resulting in a one-car accident (no injuries). Law enforcement also reported flooding on Highway 41 near Coarsegold at the same time.||This system also brought gusty winds to the west side of the San Joaquin Valley where gusts were generally 35 to 45 mph on February 6. These winds resulted in blowing dust that severely reduced visibility along Highway 41 in Kings County, closing part of the road for several hours. Wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph also occurred on February 8 into the early morning of February 9, 2015 with a few gusts to near 60 mph through the Pacheco Pass and at the Panoche Road RAWS.  Winds were also gusty across the Kern County mountains and over the high elevations of the Sierra in Tulare County with gusts 40 to 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusts of 35 to 45 mph caused areas of blowing dust along and near Highway 41 in Kings County. The dust severely reduced visibility, resulting in closure of Highway 41 between Jackson and Kansas Avenues.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of dry troughs of low pressure moved across California in the last few days of the March. The trough that moved through on March 31, increased the surface pressure gradients resulting in strong, gusty winds across the mountains and desert areas of Kern County, along with breezy conditions in the San Joaquin Valley. Winds were west-northwesterly 15-30 mph with gusts generally 45-55 mph across the mountains and desert areas. However, there were some gusts of 58-66 mph in a few wind-prone locations. The gusty winds continued into April 1st and 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bird Spring Pass RAWS reported sustained winds 35-45 mph with gusts of 58-66 mph for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated around 45 mph brought down trees and branches which caused numerous power outages in Shasta County and northern Tehama County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Locally strong winds caused numerous trees to fall on buildings, vehicles, and power lines in southern Redding. About 20 calls for separate wind damage events were received by emergency responders. At least 4 homes and a garage were struck by falling trees, some seriously damaged. Several cars were also damaged by falling trees and limbs. Power lines were downed in multiple locations, causing power outages for about 600 customers. One power line was reported to have caused a structural fire. Drought stressed trees were reported to be a significant factor in the damage by power company crews.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The long term drought continues as April was yet another below normal month for precipitation for much of the area. There was some mountain snowfall, but this did little to improve the snow pack, which remained at the lowest levels on record. By the end of April, the snow pack was only about 4 percent of normal levels. As a result, reservoirs across the area by the end of April remained well below normal levels with little or no spring rise, due to the lack of snow melt.||Extreme drought was detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor across most the region, with exceptional drought for the northern San Joaquin Valley, the central and southern Sacramento Valley, the Burney Basin and the Sierra south of Interstate 80.| |Governor Jerry Brown declared a drought emergency for the entire state of California January 17, 2014 and this continued to be in effect.  ||Local Emergency Proclamations have been issued for El Dorado, Glenn, Lake, Plumas,  San Joaquin, Shasta, Sutter,  Tuolumne, and Yuba counties. The cities of Live Oak Lodi, and Ripon continue in a drought emergency. Drought task forces have been established in Butte, Lake, Nevada, Placer, Plumas, Sacramento, San Joaquin, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tehama, Tuolumne, and Yolo counties to coordinate response to the drought.||Mandatory water reductions of 25 percent as ordered by Governor Brown were in effect statewide, the first time such mandatory restrictions have been enacted.||Agriculture has been heavily impacted, with numerous farm and farm related jobs lost. Spring planting is being scaled back significantly, with many existing tree crops and vines being removed due to a lack of irrigation water. The state continued to supply food assistance to those impacted. California Disaster Assistance Act money continued to be provided for those who have lost drinking water due to dry wells.||There was a large decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the mountains due to the lack of snow, with many resorts closing early in the month or having already closed in March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought continues to impact the southern Sacramento Valley, with farmers reducing acres planted and removing some trees from orchards. An example of this is rice acreage, which has been cut back by about 25 percent. Agriculture and related businesses have lost jobs. Air quality has suffered, with particulate levels at the highest levels in a decade for some locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowfall exceeding 8-14 inches caused travel problems in the mountains, with some light accumulations reported down to elevations around 2500 feet. Thunderstorms locally brought both large hail and large amounts of small hail, causing traffic accidents and spin outs. Multiple funnel clouds were reported, but there were no confirmed touchdowns. Heavy rainfall of 1 to 2 inches was measured, with some ponding on roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A driver along Interstate 5 saw a funnel cloud with a thunderstorm. It is unclear if it touched the ground.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter measured a storm total of 9 inches of snow, 1.12 inches of snow water equivalent.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy precipitation from a thunderstorm fell on recently burned areas in southwest Modoc County, leading to localized flash flooding and a mudslide near Day California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in Day California reported a mudslide up against her house.  This slide was associated with a burn scarred area and affected two houses.  A sheriff deputy reported that clogged culverts caused mud and water to overflow the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continues in full force during the month of November, 2015. Fresno received 1.74 inch of rainfall while Bakersfield only received 0.61 of precipitation during the month of November. The majority of this rainfall occurred in two events, one of which was fairly uniform across the area. Even with these rainfall amount there has been little response in water storage across Central California.||The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The general trend of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures has continued this year. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, a decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the Southern Sierra Nevada, reduction in air quality due to persistent stagnant air, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. Also, communities in Central California interior face state mandated reductions in municipal water use.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front pushed into the West on the 6th, bringing widespread high winds along with areas of enhanced downslope winds in the lee of the Sierra.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 61 to 70 mph were recorded at the Surprise Valley RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front pushed into the West on the 6th, bringing widespread high winds along with areas of enhanced downslope winds in the lee of the Sierra.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts largely between 50 and 60 mph were noted on the 6th, with winds in South Lake Tahoe gusting as high as 64 mph. The winds caused more than a dozen 1 to 3 foot diameter trees to be blown over or uprooted, with one tree falling on a home. The fallen trees also knocked down power lines in and near South Lake Tahoe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough of low pressure moved onshore during the day on the 13th of April resulting in onshore flow across Central California. As the low pressure system began exiting the area on the 14th the pressure gradient tightened resulting in strong, gusty winds across the mountains and desert areas of Kern County. Winds were generally westerly at 25-35 MPH across the mountains and desert ares. However, there were some gust of 50-61 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mojave National Test Pilot School's mesonet station reported strong wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely dry March followed a below normal February for most areas. This continued the 4th consecutive year of drought for the region. Mountain snowfall was very limited for the month. This along with record warmth over the area resulted in the lowest snow pack levels on record for the time of year. By the end of March, the snow pack was only about 5 percent of normal levels. Melting snow pack supplies about a third of the annual water supply for California. Reservoirs across the area by the end of March were already well below normal levels.||Extreme drought was detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor across most the region, with exceptional drought for the northern San Joaquin Valley, the central and southern Sacramento Valley, the Burney Basin and the Sierra south of Interstate 80.| |Governor Jerry Brown declared a drought emergency for the entire state of California January 17, 2014 and this continued to be in effect.  ||Local Emergency Proclamations have been issued for El Dorado, Glenn, Lake, Plumas,  San Joaquin, Shasta, Sutter,  Tuolumne, and Yuba counties. The cities of Live Oak Lodi, and Ripon continue in a drought emergency. Drought task forces have been established in Butte, Lake, Nevada, Placer, Plumas, Sacramento, San Joaquin, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tehama, Tuolumne, and Yolo counties to coordinate response to the drought.||Voluntary water conservation efforts reduced water usage by 3.6 percent statewide compared to March 2014. Governor Brown ordered mandatory restrictions of 25 percent statewide, the first time such mandatory restrictions have been enacted.||Agriculture has been heavily impacted, with numerous farm and farm related jobs lost. Spring planting is being scaled back significantly, with many existing tree crops and vines being removed due to a lack of irrigation water. The state continued to supply food assistance to those impacted. California Disaster Assistance Act money continued to be provided for those who have lost drinking water due to dry wells.||There was a large decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the mountains due to the lack of snow, with many resorts closing early.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought is strongly impacting the northern San Joaquin Valley, with farmers reducing acres planted and removing some trees from orchards. Agricultural jobs and businesses related to agriculture have lost jobs. Air quality has suffered, with particulate levels at the highest levels in a decade for some locations.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought is impacting the southern Sacramento Valley, with farmers reducing acres planted and removing some trees from orchards. An example of this is rice acreage, which has been cut back by about 25 percent. Agricultural jobs and related businesses have lost jobs. Air quality has suffered, with particulate levels at the highest levels in a decade for some locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure moving through California brought strong winds to the Kern County Mountains and Desert areas on March 23. Although most of the wind gusts were 40 to 50 mph, there were a few gusts as high as 56 mph at Jawbone RAWS and Cache Creek APRS station. At the Bird Spring Pass RAWS station, winds gusted 70 to 79 mph for several hours, while Mojave AWOS gusted to 62 mph. No damage was reported from the winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds recorded at the Bird Springs Pass RAWS station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continued in full force during the month of May, 2015. Temperatures continued to average above normal. While there was some precipitation in May, it was not uniformly distributed. Fresno received 0.57 inches of rain (133% of normal) while Bakersfield received 0.68 inches (377% of normal). Overall, seasonal rainfall amounts continued to be only 35-65 percent of normal across central California. ||The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The 2013-2014 water year (July 1 - June 30) concluded with Fresno setting its second driest on record (4.81 inches) and Bakersfield setting its third driest (2.41 inches). For the calendar year, 2014 was the warmest year on record for Fresno and Bakersfield. The trend of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures has continued through the first few months of 2015. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, a decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the Southern Sierra Nevada, reduction in air quality due to persistent stagnant air, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains.

After the rain that occurred in the San Joaquin Valley on February 5-8, 2015, stable conditions developed under a ridge of high pressure resulting in dense fog development. The fog developed after 1 am on February 11, 2015 and became dense with visibility less than 1000 feet, especially along parts of Highway 41 and Highway 99 in Kings and Fresno counties. The fog lifted after 10 am on February 11, but redeveloped shortly after midnight in February 12. The densest fog occurred around sunrise, and impacted the areas from Corcoran to Fresno, along Highways 41, 43, and 99, with numerous schools reporting bus delays due to the fog. There was also dense fog in the Merced area on the morning of February 12. As stable conditions continued, dense fog once again developed during the early morning hours of Friday, February 13, 2015. On this morning, the dense fog was more widespread, but was farther west than previous nights, generally staying west of Highway 99, but extending from west of Merced southward through the center of the San Joaquin Valley to Hanford and Corcoran. The densest fog occurred west of Fresno with several traffic accidents with unknown injuries reported. This fog pattern repeated on the morning of Saturday, February 14, 2015.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visalia, Porterville, and Tulare AWOS observations reported visibility less than 1/4 mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The persistent high pressure over region began to break down on February 21, 2015, allowing a low pressure system to drop south into Central California on Sunday, February 22, directly over the San Joaquin Valley. The storm system lingered over the area through Monday, February 23. The storm brought showers and thunderstorms to the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra Foothills. The showers and|thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall across parts of Kern County where flood|advisories were issued due to ponding of water on roadways. These advisories included the south end of the San Joaquin Valley as well as the Kern County mountain and desert areas. ||The upper level-low persisted until the 23rd, and wrap-around moisture flowed from the northeast into the east side of the San Joaquin Valley. Snow levels were fairly constant around 5000 feet over Kern and Tulare Counties during the 22nd-23rd. Around 5 to 10 inches of snow fell over the mountains of Kern and Tulare Counties, mainly during the afternoon of the 22nd and into the morning of the 23rd, and mainly above 6000 feet. A few snow showers persisted over the Sierra Nevada into the afternoon of the 23rd with a few locations in the high Sierra receiving 12 to 20 inches of snow over the two days. In addition, some instability showers and isolated thunderstorms developed in western Kern County around midday on the 23rd. One of the thunderstorms produced a brief weak tornado to the southwest of Belridge, or northwest of Taft, in the hills of the Temblor Range facing the southwestern San Joaquin Valley. There were reports of small hail about 0.25 inch in diameter reported with these thunderstorms. ||Storm total rainfall amounts during the 22nd-23rd were around 0.5 to one inch over the San Joaquin Valley from Fresno southward, up to one inch of rain fell in the desert areas, and around 1 to 1.5 inches (with some locally higher amounts) fell over the southern Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills mainly below 5000 feet. The Tehachapi Mountains received around one quarter to one-half inch of rain, including a few inches of snow above 5000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain estimated between 0.3 to 0.6 inches resulted in water flowing across State Route 33 near Twisselman Road in the vicinity of Belridge. Water was estimated to be 6 inches deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure dropped south through California on the 7th and 8th, bringing snow to Mono County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow from the afternoon of the 7th through the morning of the 8th caused the closure of the Sierra crest passes and chain controls for most of highway 395. Snowfall amounts were generally between 7 and 12 inches above 7,000 feet, with 4 to 8 inches down to around 6,000 feet. Locally higher amounts to 14 inches were noted in Lee Vining, with possible higher amounts to between 14 and 19 inches at the Sierra crest per SNOTEL observations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused flooding along the Klamath River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Klamath River at Seiad Valley exceeded the flood stage of 15.0 feet at 07/0700 PST.  The stream crested at 16.29 feet at 07/1515 PST.  The stream dropped below flood stage at 07/2115 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains brought flooding to streams and some urban areas in Siskiyou County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported along the Scott River near Callahan and near Kidder Creek.  There were also reports of farmland flooding along the Shasta River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of heavy showers and isolated thunderstorms dropped an estimated 1-4 inches of rain in south central Siskiyou county from Dunsmuir north to the Weed area.   There was some flooding as a result of this.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy showers and isolated thunderstorms dropped an estimated 1-4 inches of rain in 12-18 hours from Dunsmuir north to the Weed area.  An underground culvert was overwhelmed and water backed up into several buildings and over roads in Weed.  An emergency manager reported that the Sacramento River was beginning to overrun its banks near Dunsmuir.  Some flooding was also reported near burn scars in Weed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving through brought high winds to the Cascades and points east over Northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Van Bremmer RAWS recorded a gust to 60 mph at 05/0331 PST.  The Bolam RAWS recorded gusts of 61 mph or greater from 04/2203 PST through 05/0203 PST.  The peak gust was 67 mph, recorded at 05/0103 PST and 05/0203 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first in a series of fronts brought strong winds to many areas in Northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Collins Baldy RAWS recorded a gust to 68 mph at 05/2231 PST and to 59 mph at 05/2331 PST.  The Callahan RAWS recorded a gust to 70 mph at 05/2116 PST and to 72 mph at 05/2216 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continues in full force during the month of June, 2015. Temperatures continued to average above normal. While there was some precipitation in June, it was not uniformly distributed. Fresno received 0.01 of an inch of rain, while Bakersfield received 0.08 inches. Seasonal rainfall amounts for the lower elevations were 0 to 50 percent of normal, while the higher elevations were generally around normal for the month. ||The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The 2013-2014 water year (July 1 - June 30) concluded with Fresno setting its second driest on record (4.81 inches) and Bakersfield setting its third driest (2.41 inches). For the calendar year, 2014 was the warmest year on record for Fresno and Bakersfield. The trend of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures has continued through the first few months of 2015. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, a decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the Southern Sierra Nevada, reduction in air quality due to persistent stagnant air, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. Also, communities in Central California interior face state mandated reductions in municipal water use.

The Sky wildfire started on June 18, 2015. The fire was located 2 miles northeast of Yosemite Forks in Madera County. It burnt 500 acres. There were no fatalities or structures lost. The cost of containment was $4.9 million.

The Corrine wildfire started on June 18, 2015 and was contained on June 25, 2015. The fire was located 2 miles north of Auberry in Madera County. It burnt 920 acres and three out buildings were lost. The cost of the wildfire is unknown.

A low pressure system dropping south along the California coast brought showers and thunderstorms to central California March 1st and 2nd. Although many areas received some rain, the greatest impacts from the storm were across Kern county. Parts of Kern county received 0.25 inches to 0.75 inches of rain, especially the area in the lower Kern River Canyon east of Bakersfield. This resulted in minor rock and mud slides onto Highway 178, closing the road for several hours. On the west side of Kern county, rain was a contributing factor the the development of a sink hole in the city of Taft.||Snow also fell across the mountains of Kern County during the early hours of March 2nd. Snowfall amounts ranged from 1 inch to 5.5 inches at elevations above 5000 feet. Since snow levels remained above the level of the major highways, no significant impacts were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall on 0.74 inches at Democrat RAWS with the heaviest rain between 0225 and 0625 PST. This resulted in several small rock slides being reported at 0647 PST. The rock slides resulted in Highway 178 being closed for several hours to allow crews to clear the road.

Heavy rainfall occurred over the community of Coalinga during the afternoon hours resulting in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Both Coalinga fire department and a NWS employee reported street flooding between 1830 and 1850 PDT. Storms continued to redevelop over the area so flood threat continued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low was center near Central California, which increased cloud cover. As the low pushed off to the east during the day thunderstorms began to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported one inch hail just to the north of Ford City at 1715 PDT. Also, received a report via Facebook after the event showing the one inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low was center near Central California, which increased cloud cover. As the low pushed off to the east during the day thunderstorms began to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately 1.5 inches of rainfall fell over the cities of Taft and Maricopa in 30 minutes or so. This resulted in some flooding of an apartment complex in the city of Taft as well as a vehicles being stranded on flooded roadways. Also, SR 166 was closed around 7 PM due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level low produced some strong thunderstorms developed over Central California. This upper low had also produced some showers and thunderstorms in days prior leading to antecedent conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over two inches of rain fell over Tehachapi during the afternoon of the 12th. This resulted in a mud/debris flow near highway 58 and highway 202 in the center of Tehachapi, which closed the roadway for a couple of hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low moving out of the area after days of shower and thunderstorm activity brought a final round of storms. The main focus of storm development was across the higher elevations to include the Kern County Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall occurred across the mountains in Kern County which lead to a mud/debris flow across S. Kelso Valley road and Zimmerman St. in rural Kern County. This debris flow removed a 20 foot section of the roadway, though cost of the damage was not reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hazardous surf conditions claimed a life on a beach in Monterey County due to sneaker waves.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two women were swept into the ocean by a sneaker wave on Monastery Beach in Monterey County near Carmel. Bystanders pulled both women from the water and attempted CPR. One of the women was unresponsive and was taken to a local hospital by State Parks personnel. She was pronounced dead due to drowning.

After the rain that occurred in the San Joaquin Valley on February 5-8, 2015, stable conditions developed under a ridge of high pressure resulting in dense fog development. The fog developed after 1 am on February 11, 2015 and became dense with visibility less than 1000 feet, especially along parts of Highway 41 and Highway 99 in Kings and Fresno counties. The fog lifted after 10 am on February 11, but redeveloped shortly after midnight in February 12. The densest fog occurred around sunrise, and impacted the areas from Corcoran to Fresno, along Highways 41, 43, and 99, with numerous schools reporting bus delays due to the fog. There was also dense fog in the Merced area on the morning of February 12. As stable conditions continued, dense fog once again developed during the early morning hours of Friday, February 13, 2015. On this morning, the dense fog was more widespread, but was farther west than previous nights, generally staying west of Highway 99, but extending from west of Merced southward through the center of the San Joaquin Valley to Hanford and Corcoran. The densest fog occurred west of Fresno with several traffic accidents with unknown injuries reported. This fog pattern repeated on the morning of Saturday, February 14, 2015.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread visibility less than 1/4 mile, including the ASOS at Hanford. Several school bus delays.

A series of dry troughs of low pressure moved across California in the last few days of the March. The trough that moved through on March 31, increased the surface pressure gradients resulting in strong, gusty winds across the mountains and desert areas of Kern County, along with breezy conditions in the San Joaquin Valley. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After the rain that occurred in the San Joaquin Valley on February 5-8, 2015, stable conditions developed under a ridge of high pressure resulting in dense fog development. The fog developed after 1 am on February 11, 2015 and became dense with visibility less than 1000 feet, especially along parts of Highway 41 and Highway 99 in Kings and Fresno counties. The fog lifted after 10 am on February 11, but redeveloped shortly after midnight in February 12. The densest fog occurred around sunrise, and impacted the areas from Corcoran to Fresno, along Highways 41, 43, and 99, with numerous schools reporting bus delays due to the fog. There was also dense fog in the Merced area on the morning of February 12. As stable conditions continued, dense fog once again developed during the early morning hours of Friday, February 13, 2015. On this morning, the dense fog was more widespread, but was farther west than previous nights, generally staying west of Highway 99, but extending from west of Merced southward through the center of the San Joaquin Valley to Hanford and Corcoran. The densest fog occurred west of Fresno with several traffic accidents with unknown injuries reported. This fog pattern repeated on the morning of Saturday, February 14, 2015.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas visibility less than 1/4 mile. Several school bus delays.

An upper level trough of low pressure moved onshore on April 14th resulting in wind gusts of 45-60 MPH. An area of dust and dirt was lifted into the atmosphere, reducing visibility to near zero across Highway 180 near Fresno. The reduced visibility lead to a seven-car crash causing minor injuries and a closure of the roadway for a few hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blowing dust reduced visibility to nearly zero along Highway 180, near Fresno, causing two multi-car accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved onshore across Northern California the morning of the 7th. The associated cold front moved through the area during the day resulting in a tightening of the surface pressure gradient, which brought gusty winds of up to 49 MPH to the area. This system brought ample amounts of moisture with it which allowed for thunderstorm development during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of a funnel cloud near Madera, CA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper low off the Central California coast produced scattered showers and thunderstorms across the southern and eastern portions of the San Francisco Bay Area.  These showers and thunderstorms produced small hail. In addition, two funnel clouds were sighted in San Jose. The first was sighted at 9:20 am along highway 101 in San Jose. The second around 1:30 pm in East San Jose. Neither funnel cloud touched down and no damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public recorded a video of a funnel cloud along highway 101 in San Jose and sent the video to a local news station in San Jose. The news station forwarded the video to the San Francisco Bay Area NWS office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low moved across the northern Sierra-Nevada Range, bringing breezy winds and scattered thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NWS owned APRS station at Sunflower Valley recorded a 63 MPH gust.

The persistent high pressure over region began to break down on February 21, 2015, allowing a low pressure system to drop south into Central California on Sunday, February 22, directly over the San Joaquin Valley. The storm system lingered over the area through Monday, February 23. The storm brought showers and thunderstorms to the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra Foothills. The showers and|thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall across parts of Kern County where flood|advisories were issued due to ponding of water on roadways. These advisories included the south end of the San Joaquin Valley as well as the Kern County mountain and desert areas. ||The upper level-low persisted until the 23rd, and wrap-around moisture flowed from the northeast into the east side of the San Joaquin Valley. Snow levels were fairly constant around 5000 feet over Kern and Tulare Counties during the 22nd-23rd. Around 5 to 10 inches of snow fell over the mountains of Kern and Tulare Counties, mainly during the afternoon of the 22nd and into the morning of the 23rd, and mainly above 6000 feet. A few snow showers persisted over the Sierra Nevada into the afternoon of the 23rd with a few locations in the high Sierra receiving 12 to 20 inches of snow over the two days. In addition, some instability showers and isolated thunderstorms developed in western Kern County around midday on the 23rd. One of the thunderstorms produced a brief weak tornado to the southwest of Belridge, or northwest of Taft, in the hills of the Temblor Range facing the southwestern San Joaquin Valley. There were reports of small hail about 0.25 inch in diameter reported with these thunderstorms. ||Storm total rainfall amounts during the 22nd-23rd were around 0.5 to one inch over the San Joaquin Valley from Fresno southward, up to one inch of rain fell in the desert areas, and around 1 to 1.5 inches (with some locally higher amounts) fell over the southern Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills mainly below 5000 feet. The Tehachapi Mountains received around one quarter to one-half inch of rain, including a few inches of snow above 5000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado spotted and photographed by the public. Estimated width 50 Yards with an estimated length of 1/2  mile. The duration of the tornado was estimated at 10 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of dry troughs of low pressure moved across California in the last few days of the March. The trough that moved through on March 31, increased the surface pressure gradients resulting in strong, gusty winds across the mountains and desert areas of Kern County, along with breezy conditions in the San Joaquin Valley. Winds were west-northwesterly 15-30 mph with gusts generally 45-55 mph across the mountains and desert areas. However, there were some gusts of 58-66 mph in a few wind-prone locations. The gusty winds continued into April 1st and 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at Mojave measured sustained winds 36-43 mph along with gusts up to 60 mph.

This system brought ample amounts of moisture with it which allowed for thunderstorm development during the afternoon and evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continued in full force during the month of May, 2015. Temperatures continued to average above normal. While there was some precipitation in May, it was not uniformly distributed. Fresno received 0.57 inches of rain (133% of normal) while Bakersfield received 0.68 inches (377% of normal). The trend of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures has continued through the first few months of 2015. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. After the rain that occurred in the San Joaquin Valley on February 5-8, 2015, stable conditions developed under a ridge of high pressure resulting in dense fog development. The fog developed after 1 am on February 11, 2015 and became dense with visibility less than 1000 feet, especially along parts of Highway 41 and Highway 99 in Kings and Fresno counties. The fog lifted after 10 am on February 11, but redeveloped shortly after midnight in February 12. The densest fog occurred around sunrise, and impacted the areas from Corcoran to Fresno, along Highways 41, 43, and 99, with numerous schools reporting bus delays due to the fog. There was also dense fog in the Merced area on the morning of February 12. As stable conditions continued, dense fog once again developed during the early morning hours of Friday, February 13, 2015. On this morning, the dense fog was more widespread, but was farther west than previous nights, generally staying west of Highway 99, but extending from west of Merced southward through the center of the San Joaquin Valley to Hanford and Corcoran. The densest fog occurred west of Fresno with several traffic accidents with unknown injuries reported. This fog pattern repeated on the morning of Saturday, February 14, 2015.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hanford ASOS reported visibility less than 1/4 mile for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonable cutoff low brought a moist and unstable air mass across Southern California. Widespread thunderstorm activity was observed. Many of these storms were very strong, producing large hail, heavy rain, flash flooding as well as significant mud and debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather spotter in Leona Valley reported golf ball-size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong north to south pressure gradients generate gusty northerly winds across the mountains of Los Angeles county. Sustained winds over 40 MPH and gusts over 60 MPH were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The RAWS sensor at Malibu Hills reported sustained north winds of 43 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain over the White Mountains caused flash flooding over highway 6 and in the town of Chalfant.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain upstream in the White Mountains caused flash flooding, both on highway 6 with mud and debris over the road (causing a closure), and in the town of Chalfant with 20 homes damaged from flood waters. Four or 5 of those homes (on Sacramento St) suffered major damage per a Cal OES report. The timing of the flash flood is estimated from between the time highway 6 was reported to have debris over it until about one hour after flooding reached the town of Chalfant (times on CHP incident page).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The long term drought continued through October with little change. Reservoirs across the area were continuing to drop well below normal levels. All major reservoirs across the state were less that 40% of capacity. Folsom Lake was down to 16% of capacity, approaching near all time record low levels, set in 1977. ||Extreme drought continued to be detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor across most the region, with exceptional drought for the northern San Joaquin Valley, the central and southern Sacramento Valley, the Burney Basin and the Sierra. ||Governor Jerry Brown declared a drought emergency for the entire state of California January 17, 2014 and this continued to be in effect. ||Local Emergency Proclamations have been issued for El Dorado, Glenn, Lake, Plumas, San Joaquin, Shasta, Sutter, Tuolumne, and Yuba counties. The cities of Live Oak Lodi, and Ripon continue in a drought emergency. Drought task forces have been established in Butte, Lake, Nevada, Placer, Plumas, Sacramento, San Joaquin, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tehama, Tuolumne, and Yolo counties to coordinate response to the drought. ||Mandatory water reductions of 25 percent as ordered by Governor Brown continue to be in effect statewide, the first time such mandatory restrictions have been enacted. The mandatory reductions were missed in October, the first time since enforcement began in June 2015. Reductions of only 22 percent were achieved.||Agriculture has been heavily impacted, with numerous farm and farm related jobs lost. The UC Davis Center for Watershed Sciences released a new report indicating that California���s ongoing drought will impact the state���s agricultural economy this year by an estimated $2.7 billion and eliminate more than 21,000 jobs. The state continued to supply food assistance to those impacted, along with California Disaster Assistance Act money for those who have lost drinking water due to dry wells. Numerous dry wells have been reported across the Central Valley. Approximately 743,642 boxes of food were distributed to 391,003 households in affected communities that suffered high levels of unemployment from the drought. Nonprofit organizations in the San Joaquin Valley were struggling to meet the needs of their clients for drought-related programs. One area crop which is being heavily impacted is rice production, which is estimated to have fallen by about 25-30 percent due to water shortages. ||The continued dry weather and periods of hot temperatures left vegetation parched and ready to burn. Drought stressed and killed many trees in California���s forests, with as much as 20 percent of the state���s forests, or about 120 million trees, potentially dying from drought stress. An earlier survey by the Forest Service revealed an estimated 12.5 million dead trees in the state���s central and southern forests. This lead Gov. Jerry Brown to declare a state of emergency and ask the federal government to assist with the safe removal of dead and dying trees.||Low runoff in streams and rivers continued to cause adverse affects on fish and other wildlife. This includes the endangered Delta Smelt and winter run Chinook Salmon. The Chinook Salmon lost an estimate 95 percent of hatchlings on the Sacramento River due to the effects of the drought, and may be in danger of extinction. This is also impacting the California salmon fishing industry, which produces an estimated $1.4 billion annually.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California ski resorts continued to offer incentives, such as discounts or guarantees to increase sales of season passes, which have been hurt in recent years by poor snow conditions. Dry conditions resulted in a continued loss of trees, numbering in the millions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two hikers on the Mount Whitney Trail were treated for hypothermia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two hikers attempting a day hike on the Mount Whitney Trail were rescued and treated for hypothermia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low fueled thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert. Many storms produced flash flooding, and isolated severe weather also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rocks and mud covered Highway 168 just east of Death Valley Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low fueled thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert. Many storms produced flash flooding, and isolated severe weather also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several rooms at the Furnace Creek Inn were flooded. Many roads were closed, including Daylight Pass Road, Badwater Road, Highway 190, and Scotty's Castle Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level low pressure system moved onshore during the previous day. This system was enhanced by subtropical moisture, which helped increase the threat of heavy rainfall during the day. Afternoon thunderstorm activity was anticipated due to heating and orographic lift.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous social media and later TV media reported mudslide along Highway 58. The area had experienced some heavy rainfall through out the afternoon. Then a heavy convective shower developed near a burnt area from years prior. This allowed a large debris flow to develop and move into the Tehachapi area. This area is a known alluvial basin that can develop quick flowing flash floods, but the amount of mud and debris with this event was on the order of four to six feet deep. This trapped multiple vehicles and closed the roadway for multiple days. One individual was reported later to have been swept away during the event, with no recovery made to date.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continues in full force during the month of November, 2015. Fresno received 1.74 inch of rainfall while Bakersfield only received 0.61 of precipitation during the month of November. The majority of this rainfall occurred in two events, one of which was fairly uniform across the area. Even with these rainfall amount there has been little response in water storage across Central California.||The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The general trend of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures has continued this year. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. Also, communities in Central California interior face state mandated reductions in municipal water use.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The long term drought continued through October with little change. Reservoirs across the area were continuing to drop well below normal levels. All major reservoirs across the state were less that 40% of capacity. Folsom Lake was down to 16% of capacity, approaching near all time record low levels, set in 1977. ||Extreme drought continued to be detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor across most the region, with exceptional drought for the northern San Joaquin Valley, the central and southern Sacramento Valley, the Burney Basin and the Sierra. ||Governor Jerry Brown declared a drought emergency for the entire state of California January 17, 2014 and this continued to be in effect. ||Local Emergency Proclamations have been issued for El Dorado, Glenn, Lake, Plumas, San Joaquin, Shasta, Sutter, Tuolumne, and Yuba counties. The cities of Live Oak Lodi, and Ripon continue in a drought emergency. Drought task forces have been established in Butte, Lake, Nevada, Placer, Plumas, Sacramento, San Joaquin, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tehama, Tuolumne, and Yolo counties to coordinate response to the drought. ||Mandatory water reductions of 25 percent as ordered by Governor Brown continue to be in effect statewide, the first time such mandatory restrictions have been enacted. The mandatory reductions were missed in October, the first time since enforcement began in June 2015. Reductions of only 22 percent were achieved.||Agriculture has been heavily impacted, with numerous farm and farm related jobs lost. The UC Davis Center for Watershed Sciences released a new report indicating that California���s ongoing drought will impact the state���s agricultural economy this year by an estimated $2.7 billion and eliminate more than 21,000 jobs. The state continued to supply food assistance to those impacted, along with California Disaster Assistance Act money for those who have lost drinking water due to dry wells. Numerous dry wells have been reported across the Central Valley. Approximately 743,642 boxes of food were distributed to 391,003 households in affected communities that suffered high levels of unemployment from the drought. Nonprofit organizations in the San Joaquin Valley were struggling to meet the needs of their clients for drought-related programs. One area crop which is being heavily impacted is rice production, which is estimated to have fallen by about 25-30 percent due to water shortages. ||The continued dry weather and periods of hot temperatures left vegetation parched and ready to burn. Drought stressed and killed many trees in California���s forests, with as much as 20 percent of the state���s forests, or about 120 million trees, potentially dying from drought stress. An earlier survey by the Forest Service revealed an estimated 12.5 million dead trees in the state���s central and southern forests. This lead Gov. Jerry Brown to declare a state of emergency and ask the federal government to assist with the safe removal of dead and dying trees.||Low runoff in streams and rivers continued to cause adverse affects on fish and other wildlife. This includes the endangered Delta Smelt and winter run Chinook Salmon. The Chinook Salmon lost an estimate 95 percent of hatchlings on the Sacramento River due to the effects of the drought, and may be in danger of extinction. This is also impacting the California salmon fishing industry, which produces an estimated $1.4 billion annually.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Agriculture continued to be impacted in October with a reduced harvest, bringing a significant loss of jobs. The Household Water Supply Shortage Reporting System had 2 homes without well water in Shasta County, and 34 in Tehama County. Waterfowl faced greater risk for disease as they crowded together in fewer rice fields and wetlands. Tree mortality across the Valley increased, including even normally drought resistant oak trees. While many people reduced landscape watering at homes, water conservation in California dipped to 22 percent in October as warmer temperatures likely led to more lawn watering, compared to the benchmark year of 2013. This made October the first month that conservation fell below the mandated 25 percent since enforcement started in June.|The Household Water Supply Shortage Reporting System showed Shasta County had 2 dry wells, Tehama County 34.|Wildlife such as the winter-run Chinook Salmon in the Sacramento River suffered catastrophic losses, with the National Marine Fisheries Service reporting in October that 95 percent of the winter-run Chinook eggs, hatchlings and juvenile salmon died this year in the Sacramento River due to changes from the drought, such as an increase in water temperature.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Agriculture continued to be impacted in October with a reduced harvest, bringing a significant loss of jobs. Rice production was estimated to have dropped by 25-30 percent in the Sacramento Valley. Waterfowl faced greater risk for disease as they crowded together in fewer rice fields and wetlands. The Household Water Supply Shortage Reporting System showed 1 home without well water in Sacramento County, 8 in Yolo County. Tree mortality across the Valley increased, including even normally drought resistant oak trees. While many people reduced landscape watering at homes, water conservation in California dipped to 22 percent in October as warmer temperatures likely led to more lawn watering, compared to the benchmark year of 2013. This made October the first month that conservation fell below the mandated 25 percent since enforcement started in June. Wildlife such as the winter-run Chinook Salmon in the Sacramento River suffered catastrophic losses, with the National Marine Fisheries Service reporting in October that 95 percent of the winter-run Chinook eggs, hatchlings and juvenile salmon died this year in the Sacramento River due to changes from the drought, such as an increase in water temperature. Folsom Reservoir dropped to near record low levels, with surrounding communities relying increasingly on ground water supplies.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms associated with an upper level low off the southern California coast moved slowly north along the western Imperial county line during the early morning hours on October 15th. Some of the storms generated gusty and damaging winds which affected areas along the western side of the Salton Sea. Thunderstorm outflow winds estimated to be at least 60 mph in strength blew down over 10 power poles several miles to the northwest of the town of Salton City. No injuries were reported due to the strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed just to the west of the Salton Sea during the early morning hours on October 15th, and they moved quickly towards the north-northeast. As the storms raced to the northeast they produced gusty and damaging outflow winds estimated to be at least 60 mph in strength. According to the California Department of Highways, at about 0040PST, gusty outflow winds blew down between 10 and 15 power poles along State Route 86 at the Black Diamond Road. The damaged power poles were located about 6 miles northwest of the town of Salton City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure over the great basin forced Santa Ana Winds, with the strongest winds occurring in the Santa Ana Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Santiago Peak mesonet station recorded a wind gust of 71 mph between 530PST and 600PST with sustained winds near or above 50 mph between 400PST and 730PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From the 12th to the 20th of October, an upper low traversed the southwest in a large loop, passing over Southern California twice. This period saw scattered showers and isolated thunderstorms. The majority of activity stayed below severe limits, but a brief landspout was reported near Menifee on the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports through the broadcast media and social media of a brief landspout near Menifee. The funnel was nearly stationary and occurred over open terrain, with no damage reported.
