EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system brought a round of thunderstorms to the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Significant flash flooding occurred, along with isolated severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flash flooding hit the Grapevine Canyon area of Death Valley National Park. The Mesquite Springs Campground and Grapevine Ranger Station were evacuated; eight vehicles full of visitors and three park rangers were stranded overnight near Ubehebe Crater. Trenches up to six feet deep were cut into Scotty's Castle Road in Grapevine Canyon. 24 power poles were downed, the water treatment facility was heavily damaged, and water lines and the historic water tank were destroyed. The stables building and long house were heavily damaged. Two feet of mud and debris filled the visitor's center. Several inches of mud, rock, and debris also entered the cookhouse. Scotty's Castle measured 2.72 inches of rain in roughly five hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The long term drought continued through October with little change. Reservoirs across the area were continuing to drop well below normal levels. All major reservoirs across the state were less that 40% of capacity. Folsom Lake was down to 16% of capacity, approaching near all time record low levels, set in 1977. ||Extreme drought continued to be detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor across most the region, with exceptional drought for the northern San Joaquin Valley, the central and southern Sacramento Valley, the Burney Basin and the Sierra. ||Governor Jerry Brown declared a drought emergency for the entire state of California January 17, 2014 and this continued to be in effect. ||Local Emergency Proclamations have been issued for El Dorado, Glenn, Lake, Plumas, San Joaquin, Shasta, Sutter, Tuolumne, and Yuba counties. The cities of Live Oak Lodi, and Ripon continue in a drought emergency. Drought task forces have been established in Butte, Lake, Nevada, Placer, Plumas, Sacramento, San Joaquin, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tehama, Tuolumne, and Yolo counties to coordinate response to the drought. ||Mandatory water reductions of 25 percent as ordered by Governor Brown continue to be in effect statewide, the first time such mandatory restrictions have been enacted. The mandatory reductions were missed in October, the first time since enforcement began in June 2015. Reductions of only 22 percent were achieved.||Agriculture has been heavily impacted, with numerous farm and farm related jobs lost. The UC Davis Center for Watershed Sciences released a new report indicating that California���s ongoing drought will impact the state���s agricultural economy this year by an estimated $2.7 billion and eliminate more than 21,000 jobs. The state continued to supply food assistance to those impacted, along with California Disaster Assistance Act money for those who have lost drinking water due to dry wells. Numerous dry wells have been reported across the Central Valley. Approximately 743,642 boxes of food were distributed to 391,003 households in affected communities that suffered high levels of unemployment from the drought. Nonprofit organizations in the San Joaquin Valley were struggling to meet the needs of their clients for drought-related programs. One area crop which is being heavily impacted is rice production, which is estimated to have fallen by about 25-30 percent due to water shortages. ||The continued dry weather and periods of hot temperatures left vegetation parched and ready to burn. Drought stressed and killed many trees in California���s forests, with as much as 20 percent of the state���s forests, or about 120 million trees, potentially dying from drought stress. An earlier survey by the Forest Service revealed an estimated 12.5 million dead trees in the state���s central and southern forests. This lead Gov. Jerry Brown to declare a state of emergency and ask the federal government to assist with the safe removal of dead and dying trees.||Low runoff in streams and rivers continued to cause adverse affects on fish and other wildlife. This includes the endangered Delta Smelt and winter run Chinook Salmon. The Chinook Salmon lost an estimate 95 percent of hatchlings on the Sacramento River due to the effects of the drought, and may be in danger of extinction. This is also impacting the California salmon fishing industry, which produces an estimated $1.4 billion annually.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Agriculture continued to be impacted in October with a reduced harvest, bringing a significant loss of jobs. Rice production was estimated to have dropped by 25-30 percent in the Sacramento Valley. The Household Water Supply Shortage Reporting System showed 70 homes in Butte County were without well water, 27 in Glenn County, 8 in Colusa County. Wildlife such as the winter-run Chinook Salmon in the Sacramento River suffered catastrophic losses, with the National Marine Fisheries Service reporting in October that 95 percent of the winter-run Chinook eggs, hatchlings and juvenile salmon died this year in the Sacramento River due to changes from the drought, such as an increase in water temperature.. Waterfowl faced greater risk for disease as they crowded together in fewer rice fields and wetlands. Tree mortality across the Valley increased, including even normally drought resistant oak trees. While many people reduced landscape watering at homes, water conservation in California dipped to 22 percent in October as warmer temperatures likely led to more lawn watering, compared to the benchmark year of 2013. This made October the first month that conservation fell below the mandated 25 percent since enforcement started in June. Wildlife such as the winter-run Chinook Salmon in the Sacramento River suffered catastrophic losses, with the National Marine Fisheries Service reporting in October that 95 percent of the winter-run Chinook eggs, hatchlings and juvenile salmon died this year in the Sacramento River due to changes from the drought, such as an increase in water temperature. Folsom Reservoir dropped to near record low levels, with surrounding communities relying increasingly on ground water supplies.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies and a cool air mass allowed temperatures to drop below freezing at a few places in northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures ranged from 24 degrees in Fort Jones to 39 degrees in Happy Camp and Callahan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies and a cool air mass allowed temperatures to drop below freezing at a few places in northern Californiak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures ranged from 23 to 25 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued drought, with periods of heat, breezy winds and low humidity increased wildfire danger through early August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lowell Fire started on July 25th, with most fire growth and damage occurring in July. The cause is still under investigation. There were a total of 2304 acres burned, with a total of 6 firefighters injured (occurred and documented in July). Two firefighters from the US Forest Service and 2 from CAL Fire suffered burns while fighting the fire. One of the firefighters had serious burns and required further hospitalization. There were 2 structures destroyed, 1 damaged. Wildfire suppression costs were estimated to be $18 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance combined with monsoonal moisture to spread showers and isolated strong thunderstorms into the northern Sierra.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to the size of nickels was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued drought, with periods of heat, and low humidity continued to enhance wildfire danger through much of August, especially in the Coastal Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rocky Fire started on July 29th, and burned 50,000 additional acres between August 1-14. A total of 69,438 acres were burned, with 43 residences, and 53 outbuildings destroyed. There were also 8 structures damaged. Cal Fire law enforcement said a faulty gas water heater in an outbuilding was blamed for the Rocky Fire. |The Jerusalem Fire started to the south on August 9th, and ultimately merged with the Rocky Fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued drought, with periods of heat, and low humidity continued to enhance wildfire danger through much of August, especially in the Coastal Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jerusalem Fire started on August 9th, and burned 25,118 acres, with 6 residences, and 21 outbuildings destroyed. The cause is still under investigation.|The Jerusalem Fire merged with the larger Rocky Fire to the north.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Agriculture continued to be impacted in August bringing a significant loss of jobs. Tree mortality across the Valley increased, including even normally drought resistant oak trees. Golf courses faced pressure to use less water and were doing so, bringing a dry, brown appearance to the turf, some remained shut down due to a lack of water. Many people reduced landscape watering at homes or removed grass lawns. Grass fires enhanced by the dry conditions were common.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The long term drought continued through August with little change. Without a snow pack for late spring/early summer, reservoirs across the area by the end of the month were continuing to drop well below normal levels. All major reservoirs across the state were less that 40% of capacity by the end of the month. Folsom Lake was down to 20% of capacity, approaching near-record low levels for August, seen last in 1977. ||Extreme drought continued to be detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor across most the region, with exceptional drought for the northern San Joaquin Valley, the central and southern Sacramento Valley, the Burney Basin and the Sierra. ||The continued dry weather and periods of hot temperatures left vegetation parched and ready to burn. Wildfire activity was well above normal levels. ||Governor Jerry Brown declared a drought emergency for the entire state of California January 17, 2014 and this continued to be in effect. ||Local Emergency Proclamations have been issued for El Dorado, Glenn, Lake, Plumas, San Joaquin, Shasta, Sutter, Tuolumne, and Yuba counties. The cities of Live Oak Lodi, and Ripon continue in a drought emergency. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought continues to impact agriculture in the Delta. Wildlife such as the endangered Delta smelt and winter run Chinook Salmon are suffering losses due to changes from the drought, such as an increase in water temperature and salinity levels. Wildfires were enhanced, such as the Jerusalem Fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Frog Wildfire was started by lightning at around 1645 PST on 07/30/2015.  As of 0330 PST on 08/01/2015, the fire covered 1000 acres and was 10 percent contained.  400,000 dollars had been spent on firefighting efforts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Frog Wildfire was started by lightning at around 1645 PST on 07/30/2015.  As of 0330 PST on 08/01/2015, the fire covered 1000 acres and was 10 percent contained.  400,000 dollars had been spent on firefighting efforts. On the evening of July 30, 2015, 38 year-old Black Hills National Forest Engine Captain, David ���Dave��� Ruhl, while scouting on foot during initial attack of the Frog Fire on the Modoc National Forest, was entrapped and killed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical moisture from the remnants of Hurricane Dolores and a monsoonal fetch of moisture streamed into southern and central California during the time period. This brought deep moisture into the area which resulted in moderate to heavy rainfall across most of the southern San Joaquin Valley and nearly all of Kern County. Kern County estimates the cost of the emergency response and infrastructure repair  throughout the Kern Operational Area to be approximately 2.7 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Debris flow from over one inch of rain in less than a hour over the Grand Fire burn scar area produced mud blocking the road between Frazier Park and Lake of the Woods.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance combined with monsoonal moisture to spread showers and isolated strong thunderstorms into the northern Sierra.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to 1 inch in diameter reported in Dardanelle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very moist and unstable conditions were present across Joshua Tree National Park on July 19th, due in large part to a significant infusion of tropical moisture from former hurricane Dolores. During the afternoon hours, thunderstorms developed across much of the park, and they produced intense downpours with peak rain rates well in excess of 2 inches per hour. The very heavy rainfall led to some flash flooding in the western portion of Joshua Tree National Park which also caused debris to flow across roads near the west entrance to the park. Fortunately there were no injuries due to the flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across portions of Joshua Tree National Park during the afternoon hours on July 19th. Due to an infusion of tropical moisture from former hurricane Dolores, the storms generated torrential rainfall with peak rain rates well in excess of 2 inches per hour. The intense rainfall led to flash flooding across the western portion of the park. At about 1500PST, local law enforcement reported flash flooding of Park Boulevard, Route 12, near and just southeast of the western entrance to Joshua Tree National Park. In addition, the flooding washed debris onto portions of Park Boulevard. No injuries were reported due to the flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent moderate to high atmospheric moisture combined with daytime heating to produce thunderstorms (many with heavy rainfall) early in the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was estimated to be golf-ball sized from a Facebook image. The hail broke flower pots, a birdbath and two glass tables.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very large and cold upper-level low deepened along the West Coast before moving inland directly over southern California. This late-season system brought significant rain and snow showers over and west of the mountains on the 7th and 8th. Most of these areas saw between one-quarter of an inch and one inch of precipitation over these two days. The snow level dropped to around 3500 feet with light snow falling, but not sticking at that elevation. Above 4000 feet, snow amounts ranged from 1 to 6 inches, requiring chain control on Sunrise Highway in San Diego County. Flooding due to heavy rainfall was reported at the Spring Valley Quarry Road dip and Escondido Creek on Country Club Drive, where the roads were closed. At the beginning of the event, strong westerly winds produced gusts to 60 mph at Edom Hill and 65 mph at Whitewater RAWS, both in the Banning Pass/mountain desert slope region of Riverside County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Funnel cloud reported near San Bernardino by a local resident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical moisture left over from former Hurricane Dolores brought thunderstorms with hail and heavy rain into Northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail fell 1 mile SW of Bear Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowfall exceeding 8-14 inches caused travel problems in the mountains, with some light accumulations reported down to elevations around 2500 feet. Thunderstorms locally brought both large hail and large amounts of small hail, causing traffic accidents and spin outs. Multiple funnel clouds were reported, but there were no confirmed touchdowns. Heavy rainfall of 1 to 2 inches was measured, with some ponding on roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 24 hour total rain was 0.86 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of monsoonal moisture from northern Mexico produced strong thunderstorms across Ventura and Los Angeles counties. Heavy rain from the thunderstorms resulted in flash flooding and mud/debris flows across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms formed over north central Los Angeles county, north of the community of Acton. Heavy rain from the thunderstorms generated numerous mud/debris flows across Highway 14 between Red Rover Mine Road and Escondido Canyon Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: July rounded out with another shot of monsoon moisture and thunderstorms as high pressure shifted back westward over the Four Corners on the 29th and 30th. The main drivers for storms were terrain and outflow boundary collisions. Scattered thunderstorms occurred mainly over the mountains and deserts with wide-ranging rainfall totals from a few tenths of an inch to locally over 2 inches. Flash flooding occurred in Idyllwild, Timoteo Canyon, Calimesa/Moreno Valley, along with water flowing in the Fish and Vallecito Creeks in Anza Borrego State Park. A few storms went severe, including quarter-sized hail and wind damage in Calimesa and wind damage from a microburst in Palm Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 18 flooded, with portions of roadway washing away. Several vehicles were stuck in mud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another push of monsoon moisture fueled more thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Significant flash flooding and isolated severe weather occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Vehicles were stuck on Highway 190 near the Mesquite Sand Dunes with water half way up the tires.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Debris and water was reported as moving across the intersection of Garlock road and Redrock Randsburg road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A huge push of monsoon moisture from Hurricane Dolores fueled a major outbreak of flash flooding and severe weather in the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 168 was closed at Westgard Pass due to flooding and lots of debris in the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continues in full force during the month of September, 2015. Temperatures continued to average above normal. Fresno received 0.12 inch of rainfall while Bakersfield only received a trace of precipitation during the month of September. ||The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The general trend of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures has continued this year. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, a decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the Southern Sierra Nevada, reduction in air quality due to persistent stagnant air, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. Also, communities in Central California interior face state mandated reductions in municipal water use.

Remnant moisture from Hurricane Linda generated strong thunderstorms across Los Angeles county. Heavy rain from these thunderstorms resulted in flash flooding as well as mud/debris flows across parts of Los Angeles county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over the mountains of Los Angeles county. Heavy rain from these thunderstorms generated mud and debris flows across sections of Lake Hughes Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure dropped from the northeast Pacific into California on the 13th, bringing high winds to the Surprise Valley and heavy snow to the northern Sierra and portions of northeast California, mainly above about 5000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine inches of snow fell in Loyalton, with 10 inches estimated from the Independence Creek SNOTEL (just east of Independence Lake).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure dropped from the northeast Pacific into California on the 13th, bringing high winds to the Surprise Valley and heavy snow to the northern Sierra and portions of northeast California, mainly above about 5000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten to 20 inches of snow fell near the Sierra crest, with 7 to 9 inches around Lake Tahoe, in Woodfords, and on the slopes of the Verdi Range (west of Verdi).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist but relatively warm west to east flow across the Pacific brought very heavy high elevation snow to the Tahoe area on the 21st and 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to 4 feet of snow fell above 7000 feet around the Lake Tahoe Basin on the 21st and 22nd. There was a mix of rain and snow below 6500 to 7000 feet, with minimal snowfall at lake level (1 to 3.5 inches Tahoe City and 1/2 inch in South Lake Tahoe) with mainly rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days of precipitation in northeast California with moist flow off the Pacific wrapped up with a final heavy snowfall event on the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 8 to 10 inches of snow fell southwest of Susanville in the higher elevations per SNOTEL and HADS sensors. In the lower elevations, 5 inches of snow was noted at the Portola CO-OP station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, gusty cross winds caused a plane to crash on takeoff at the Mammoth-Yosemite airport on the 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South-southwest cross winds of 25 mph gusting to 38 mph at the Mammoth-Yosemite airport caused an experienced pilot to overcompensate and dip a wing of his Piper Mirage into the ground on takeoff. The wings and landing gear broke off. There were two people on board and the pilot suffered minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure dropped from the northeast Pacific into California on the 13th, bringing high winds to the Surprise Valley and heavy snow to the northern Sierra and portions of northeast California, mainly above about 5000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts between 59 and 65 mph occurred at the Surprise Valley RAWS on the morning of the 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Subtropical moisture from the remnants of Hurricane Linda brought widespread showers to most of the area, deserts included. The first isolated thunderstorm developed on the 6th in San Diego County as a moisture surge in monsoon warm upper level flow arrived. Intense heavy rain from thunderstorms occurred on the 7th with flash flooding in several locations including Victorville and Forest Falls. A swift water rescue in Victorville was successful, but another incident in Forest Falls had one rescue but a drowning occurred when a 30-year-old couple crossed Mill Creek and were swept away. Most areas received between a few hundredths to one-third of an inch of rain, with a few upslope areas of the San Bernardino Mountains receiving up to around one inch. Additional thunderstorms developed on the 8th by noon over the mountains and quickly spread into the Inland Empire and Orange County, as well as near I-15 in San Diego County. Locally heavy rain and several trees and poles were downed in the Riverside area including a small dust storm. Several hail reports, mostly nickel-sized, but a few larger. Activity decreased on the 9th with only isolated thunderstorms formed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CHP reported mud and rocks on Highway 78 near Yaqui Pass, closing Highway 78. CHP also reported flash flooding Highway S2 from mile marker 36 to mile marker 44, blocking the southbound lanes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnant moisture from Hurricane Linda generated strong thunderstorms across Los Angeles county. Heavy rain from these thunderstorms resulted in flash flooding as well as mud/debris flows across parts of Los Angeles county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed near the community of Llano. Heavy rain resulted in several flooded roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days of precipitation in northeast California with moist flow off the Pacific wrapped up with a final heavy snowfall event on the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five inches of snow fell in Cedarville between late evening on the 23rd and the morning of the 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days of precipitation in northeast California with moist flow off the Pacific wrapped up with a final heavy snowfall event on the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eighteen to 24 inches of snow fell at ski areas and along and west of highway 89 on the 24th. Elsewhere, a widespread 10 to 14 inches of snow fell around Lake Tahoe, in Truckee, and east to the foothills of the Verdi Range.

Most areas received between a few hundredths to one-third of an inch of rain, with a few upslope areas of the San Bernardino Mountains receiving up to around one inch. Additional thunderstorms developed on the 8th by noon over the mountains and quickly spread into the Inland Empire and Orange County, as well as near I-15 in San Diego County. Locally heavy rain and several trees and poles were downed in the Riverside area including a small dust storm. Several hail reports, mostly nickel-sized, but a few larger. Activity decreased on the 9th with only isolated thunderstorms formed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple swift water rescues along Bear Valley Road. Water flowed fast down the road with some areas of water around 5 feet deep. A spotter reported water 4-6 deep at the intersection of Bear Valley Road and Apple Valley Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A flash flood at Mill Creek crossing...40 feet wide and 8 to 12 inches deep. The head of the flash flood was 3 to 4 feet deep. From Victor Valley news group: flood waters overtook a couple hiking. An off-duty sheriff's deputy heard the couple calling for help and was able to save the young woman, but the man was swept away. The body of the victim, a Rancho Cucamonga man in his 20s, was found shortly after 1800 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper level Pacific trough moved through the West Coast on September 14-15 and tapped into remnant tropical cyclone Linda moisture west of the California Bight. Widespread and locally heavy rainfall developed across the entire CWA with 1 to 2 inches of rainfall common. The heavy rain resulted in a major traffic impact during the morning commute in LA and Orange County, along with a debris flow in Silverado Canyon, and widespread urban flooding in San Diego, Orange, Riverside and San Bernardino Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several businesses in the Midway District were flooded...with water up to knee deep. Several vehicles were flooded in an underground parking garage in Ocean Beach, including a brand new Lamborghini luxury car flooded halfway deep.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An 8.3 magnitude earthquake that struck off the coast of Chile on the 16th led the National Tsunami Warning Center to issue a tsunami advisory at 1743 PST for a swath of California, from Orange County to north of San Luis Obispo. No action was necessary in San Diego County, the Center determined. All beaches, harbors, piers, and marinas in the Cities of Seal Beach, Huntington Beach, Newport Beach, Laguna Beach, Dana Point and San Clemente, including County and State beaches were closed through 0500 PST on the 17th. Tsunami wave heights were observed to be just under one foot along the Orange County coast. The Orange County EOC reported no significant coastal flooding occurred, but to be aware of the high likelihood of strong currents and waves dangerous to persons in or near the water. The advisory was lifted around 1100 PST on the 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The long term drought continued through September with little change. Reservoirs across the area were continuing to drop well below normal levels. All major reservoirs across the state were less that 40% of capacity. Folsom Lake was down to 18% of capacity, approaching near-record low levels for September, seen last in 1977.  70 homes in Butte County are without well water. The numbers reflect cumulative reports of household water shortages by county reported to the state since January 2014.||A growing number of residential wells are running dry. The Household Water Supply Shortage Reporting System showed 70 homes in Butte County without well water. Other counties reported residential water shortages as follows: Tuolumne 333, Glenn County 27; Shasta County 2; Tehama County 34.||Extreme drought continued to be detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor across most the region, with exceptional drought for the northern San Joaquin Valley, the central and southern Sacramento Valley, the Burney Basin and the Sierra. ||The continued dry weather and periods of hot temperatures left vegetation parched and ready to burn. Wildfire activity was far above normal levels in September, with the Valley Fire becoming the 3rd most destructive wildfire in California history, and the Butte Fire the 7th most destructive.||Governor Jerry Brown declared a drought emergency for the entire state of California January 17, 2014 and this continued to be in effect. ||Local Emergency Proclamations have been issued for El Dorado, Glenn, Lake, Plumas, San Joaquin, Shasta, Sutter, Tuolumne, and Yuba counties. The cities of Live Oak Lodi, and Ripon continue in a drought emergency. Drought task forces have been established in Butte, Lake, Nevada, Placer, Plumas, Sacramento, San Joaquin, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tehama, Tuolumne, and Yolo counties to coordinate response to the drought. ||Mandatory water reductions of 25 percent as ordered by Governor Brown continued to be in effect statewide, the first time such mandatory restrictions have been enacted. ||Agriculture has been heavily impacted, with numerous farm and farm related jobs lost. The UC Davis Center for Watershed Sciences released a new report indicating that California���s ongoing drought will impact the state���s agricultural economy this year by an estimated $2.7 billion and eliminate more than 21,000 jobs. The state continued to supply food assistance to those impacted, along with California Disaster Assistance Act money for those who have lost drinking water due to dry wells. Approximately 743,642 boxes of food were distributed to 391,003 households in affected communities that suffered high levels of unemployment from the drought. Nonprofit organizations in the San Joaquin Valley were struggling to meet the needs of their clients for drought-related programs. One area crop which is being heavily impacted is rice production, which is expected to fall by about 30 percent due to water shortages.||Low runoff in streams and rivers on fish and ecological continues to cause adverse affects on forests and wildlife, including the endangered Delta Smelt and winter run Chinook Salmon. High temperatures in the Sacramento River are having a particularly strong effect on young salmon, and could repeat the major fish kill seen in the summer of 2014.||Air quality in the Valley has been poor throughout the drought, particularly in the San Joaquin Valley, due to the lack of rain to wash particles from the air and dust from unplanted fields and newly dug wells. Smoke from recent fires has worsened air quality significantly, causing problems even for healthy people. Asthma has become a much bigger problem in the Valley since the start of the drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California ski resorts were offering incentives, such as discounts or guarantees to increase sales of season passes, which have been hurt in recent years by poor snow conditions. Dry conditions resulted in a continued loss of trees, numbering potentially in the millions, with heightened wildfire activity (see Butte Fire entry).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Agriculture continued to be impacted in September during the harvest, with a significant loss of jobs. Rice production was forecast to drop by 30 percent in the Sacramento Valley. Bee keepers scaled back honey production due to a lack of nectar and honey to sustain their bees. Tree mortality across the Valley increased, including even normally drought resistant oak trees. Golf courses faced pressure to use less water and were doing so, bringing a dry, brown appearance to the turf, and some remained shut down due to a lack of water. Many people reduced landscape watering at homes or removed grass lawns. Grass fires enhanced by the dry conditions were common.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme fire behavior with winds and very dry fuels combining to enhance rapidly moving fires which destroyed hundreds of homes and caused multiple deaths in the Valley and Butte Fires. The Valley Fire is considered the 3rd most destructive wildfire in California history, the Butte Fire the 7th most destructive.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were 70,868 acres were burned in the Butte Fire, the 7th most destructive fire in California history. There were 475 residences and 343 outbuildings destroyed, and 45 structures damaged. In the fire there were 2 civilian fatalities and 1 injury. The cause has tentatively been attributed to a PG&E power line coming in contact with a live tree.  Extensive damage was done to power lines and poles. PG&E has identified 505 locations where equipment needs to be fixed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme fire behavior with winds and very dry fuels combining to enhance rapidly moving fires which destroyed hundreds of homes and caused multiple deaths in the Valley and Butte Fires. The Valley Fire is considered the 3rd most destructive wildfire in California history, the Butte Fire the 7th most destructive.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were 76,067 acres burned in the Valley Fire. The Valley Fire was the 3rd most damaging fire in California history. At least 1958 structures were destroyed including; 1280 homes, 27 multi-family structures, 66 commercial properties, and 585 other minor structures such as outbuildings and sheds. There are 93 structures that have been damaged including: 41 homes, 7 commercial properties, and 45 other minor structures. In the fire there were 4 firefighters injured, 4 civilian fatalities. The cause of the fire is under investigation. Westerly winds gusting to over 40 mph were recorded in the area, causing a rapid surge in the early hours of the fire. One theory suggests that these winds may have been caused by a down burst from showers along the coast interacting with very dry air near the surface.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Rough Fire was started by lightning on July 31, 2015 in the Kings River Drainage, 5 miles north of Hume Lake and 2.5 miles southwest of Spanish Mountain. The fire burned actively through August and September. As of September 30, it had burned 151,623 acres. The fire was burning in the Sierra National Forest, Sequoia National Forest, and Sequoia & Kings Canyon National Parks. The fire closed Highway 180 east of Grant Grove and closed all of Kings Canyon National Park for several weeks. The fire threatened infrastructure in the Hume Lake area as well as in Cedar Grove and several other small communities. There were 4 structures lost. The cost of containment as of September 30, was 118.9 million. The fire was 85 percent contained on September 30, and continued to burn in the interior areas into October.

The Tenaya fire began on the evening of September 7, 2015, in Yosemite National Park, approximately 2 miles north of Yosemite Valley. The cause is under investigation. The fire burned 415 acres before being contained on September 16. There were no structures lost. The cost of containment was $1.9 million.

A broad upper level Pacific trough moved through the West Coast on September 14-15 and tapped into remnant tropical cyclone Linda moisture west of the California Bight. Widespread and locally heavy rainfall developed across the entire CWA with 1 to 2 inches of rainfall common. The heavy rain resulted in a major traffic impact during the morning commute in LA and Orange County, along with a debris flow in Silverado Canyon, and widespread urban flooding in San Diego, Orange, Riverside and San Bernardino Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CBS affiliate in Los Angeles reported two westbound lanes on the 91 freeway at Green River Road in Corona experienced a 50-yard crack as water penetrated the soil underneath the pavement. An emergency repair of the road took place and multiple lanes were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mostly clear skies and a cool air mass made for a cold night over northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures ranged from 32 to 41 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continues in full force during the month of September, 2015. Temperatures continued to average above normal. Fresno received 0.12 inch of rainfall while Bakersfield only received a trace of precipitation during the month of September. ||The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The general trend of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures has continued this year. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, a decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the Southern Sierra Nevada, reduction in air quality due to persistent stagnant air, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. Also, communities in Central California interior face state mandated reductions in municipal water use.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Cabin fire was started by lightning on July 19, 2015 and continued to burn into August. It was located 8 mile northeast of Camp Nelson in Tulare County. The fire remained around 2 acres in size until July 30, 2015, then became active and burned 800 acres by the end of the day on July 31.

The tail-end of a round of monsoon activity added an additional tenth of an inch or less to area mountains and northern deserts, though a few spots received up to around one-third of an inch, including a portion of the Lake Fire burn area on the 6th. The rain over the fire produced debris flows, consisting mostly of ash and mud over portions of Highway 38, up to a foot deep in some areas. Some flooding and hail was also reported in the high desert near Apple Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Officials at the Lake Fire and CHP reported several debris flows; one consisting mostly of ash, 4 to 12 inches deep on Highway 38 at the South Fork of the Santa Ana River, as well as one 4 to 5 inches of mud and debris over another portion of the highway. The debris flow near the South Fork was located near one of the fire's highest impact areas, with an estimated 0.33 inches of rain falling on that basin, resulting in the debris flow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: CHP reported one foot of water flowing over Highway 395.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: July rounded out with another shot of monsoon moisture and thunderstorms as high pressure shifted back westward over the Four Corners on the 29th and 30th. The main drivers for storms were terrain and outflow boundary collisions. Scattered thunderstorms occurred mainly over the mountains and deserts with wide-ranging rainfall totals from a few tenths of an inch to locally over 2 inches. Flash flooding occurred in Idyllwild, Timoteo Canyon, Calimesa/Moreno Valley, along with water flowing in the Fish and Vallecito Creeks in Anza Borrego State Park. A few storms went severe, including quarter-sized hail and wind damage in Calimesa and wind damage from a microburst in Palm Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in Lucerne Valley reported a lightning strike onto power lines causing a power outage across a section of the Lucerne Valley area to the southeast of town. A transformer was burned out and tripped a number of circuit breakers on the nearby power grid causing the outage for over an hour in some locations.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photos shared by a local media outlet indicate a downburst/microburst was likely the cause of extensive wind damage in the northeast side of Indian Wells in the Palm Desert Country Club area. Based on damage, winds were estimated at 60 to 70 mph. Multiple large trees and power lines down, along with roofing material and other debris on the ground. Trees fell on an apartment on Elkhorn Trail and damaged the roof. A total of 5288 customers lost power due to downed lines. Fred Waring Drive between El Dorado and Warner Trail in Palm Desert was also closed due to flooding and debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Willow Fire began on July 25, 2015 and was contained on August 11, 2015. It was human caused and was located 2 miles west of Wishon, CA, in the Sierra National Forest. It burned 5702 acres. There were no structures lost and no fatalities. The cost of containment was 19.7 million.

Breezy southwest winds combined with dry fuels to intensify the development of wildfires, including the Kyburz and Wragg Fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wragg Fire burned 8050 acres in Napa and Solano counties. There were 2 outbuildings destroyed , 4 outbuildings and 1 residence damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The North fire began along Interstate 15 at the Cajon Summit in the early afternoon of the 17th. A mandatory evacuation order was issued for the Baldy Mesa area between Phelan and Hesperia. Interstate 15 was closed in both directions for hours with several cars still on the road. The fire burned rapidly on and near Interstate 15, destroying 20 vehicles and damaging 10 others, as well as destroying 5 homes. The fire burned a total of 3500 acres surrounding the interstate before being fully contained later that evening. The cause of the fire is unknown.

Considerable moisture from Hurricane Dolores to the south, along with monsoon moisture from the southeast resulted in widespread showers and thunderstorms over most of the HSA for the 17th-19th. Rainfall ranged from one-half of an inch up to around 4 inches, including a record 1.71 inches at Lindbergh Field on the 18th (July 18 single-day and July monthly total). The San Diego River at Fashion Valley had 2 crests above monitor stage, 7.7 feet on the 18th and 8.8 feet on the 19th. The heavy rain resulted in a debris flow in the burn scar of Silverado Canyon, as well as flash flooding in Moreno Valley, Perris, and La Mesa on the 19th and a wet microburst in Tierrasanta on the 18th. The rain also caused the first Los Angeles Angels baseball game rain-out since 1995, and a rare 2-hour rain delay at the San Diego Padres baseball game. Over 2000 lightning strikes reported on July 18, some starting small brush fires. Microburst wind damage occurred in Tierrasanta, along with additional damage from a haboob in the Anza Borrego SP and wind damage from a thunderstorm in Palm Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An trained spotter estimated a 60 mph wind gust just southeast of Borrego Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another push of monsoon moisture fueled more thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Significant flash flooding and isolated severe weather occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water and mud flowed over Highway 395 4 miles S of Haiwee and also near the Coso Junction Rest Area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water flooded the entire parking lot of the Amargosa Opera House and also entered the building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another push of monsoon moisture fueled an outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Several storms produced flash flooding and severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water one foot deep flowed over Reche Road at Sunny Vista.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another push of monsoon moisture fueled an outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Several storms produced flash flooding and severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters two to three feet deep flowed over Interstate 15, stopping traffic in both directions. The side of the roadway washed away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front pushed into the West on the 6th, bringing widespread high winds along with areas of enhanced downslope winds in the lee of the Sierra.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 60 to 79 mph were recorded on the 6th, with 79 mph measured at the Doyle RAWS near highway 395. The high winds caused 2 semi trucks to overturn on highway 395 between Susanville and Janesville, with visibility in that area dropping to less than 1/2 mile at times due to blowing dust. Also, a 2 foot diameter tree in Graeagle fell over and split an outbuilding in two. Finally, an aerial survey by the U.S. Forest Service estimated that over 71K trees were broken or blown over, mostly in western Lassen County but with some of that number in adjacent counties to the west and southwest. Wind directions were atypical (south to southeast) for the magnitude of the winds and this likely boosted the number of downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The last remnants of monsoon moisture from the late July push produced isolated flash flooding in eastern California and western Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water, mud, and rocks covered Highway 168 in The Narrows section.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Considerable moisture from Hurricane Dolores to the south, along with monsoon moisture from the southeast resulted in widespread showers and thunderstorms over most of the HSA for the 17th-19th. Rainfall ranged from one-half of an inch up to around 4 inches, including a record 1.71 inches at Lindbergh Field on the 18th (July 18 single-day and July monthly total). The San Diego River at Fashion Valley had 2 crests above monitor stage, 7.7 feet on the 18th and 8.8 feet on the 19th. The heavy rain resulted in a debris flow in the burn scar of Silverado Canyon, as well as flash flooding in Moreno Valley, Perris, and La Mesa on the 19th and a wet microburst in Tierrasanta on the 18th. The rain also caused the first Los Angeles Angels baseball game rain-out since 1995, and a rare 2-hour rain delay at the San Diego Padres baseball game. Over 2000 lightning strikes reported on July 18, some starting small brush fires. Microburst wind damage occurred in Tierrasanta, along with additional damage from a haboob in the Anza Borrego SP and wind damage from a thunderstorm in Palm Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six large pine trees downed by strong winds near Porto Ct. The largest tree was close to 3 feet in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The McCabe fire began in July 18, 2015 and was contained on July 20, 2015. It was located 16 miles northwest of Los Banos, CA and burned 1333 acres. There were no structures lost and no fatalities. The cost of containment was $725,000.
Another push of monsoon moisture fueled thunderstorms over the eastern portions of the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Some storms produced severe weather and flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Considerable moisture from Hurricane Dolores to the south, along with monsoon moisture from the southeast resulted in widespread showers and thunderstorms over most of the HSA for the 17th-19th. Rainfall ranged from one-half of an inch up to around 4 inches, including a record 1.71 inches at Lindbergh Field on the 18th (July 18 single-day and July monthly total). The San Diego River at Fashion Valley had 2 crests above monitor stage, 7.7 feet on the 18th and 8.8 feet on the 19th. The heavy rain resulted in a debris flow in the burn scar of Silverado Canyon, as well as flash flooding in Moreno Valley, Perris, and La Mesa on the 19th and a wet microburst in Tierrasanta on the 18th. The rain also caused the first Los Angeles Angels baseball game rain-out since 1995, and a rare 2-hour rain delay at the San Diego Padres baseball game. Over 2000 lightning strikes reported on July 18, some starting small brush fires. Microburst wind damage occurred in Tierrasanta, along with additional damage from a haboob in the Anza Borrego SP and wind damage from a thunderstorm in Palm Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CHP reported significant urban flooding in the Moreno Valley area due to a heavy line of thunderstorms. The most significant flooding was reported at the intersection of Interstate 215 and Van Buren Blvd, where 8 cars were stuck in high waters. In Reche Canyon residents were unable to leave their homes due to flooded roadways. Portions of State Highway 60 were also impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent moderate to high atmospheric moisture combined with daytime heating to produce thunderstorms (many with heavy rainfall) early in the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail size was estimated by spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent moderate to high atmospheric moisture combined with daytime heating to produce thunderstorms (many with heavy rainfall) early in the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two photographs indicated a waterspout on Lake Tahoe. Eyewitnesses noted that the waterspout started near the mouth of Emerald Bay and moved quickly southeast towards Tahoe Keys. The waterspout dissipated before reaching land.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A non-supercell tornado occurred over far southeastern Lassen County on the evening of the 20th, with a burn scar debris flow on the 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CALTRANS official reported up to 4 feet of mud and debris over highway 395 near the burn scar from the Red Rock Fire (fire occurred in 2013). It took 5 hours to clear all the debris off of the road, with one car getting stuck in the debris (necessitating a rescue).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly flow from low pressure off the California coast brought subtropical moisture into the area, generating mountain thunderstorms. Strong winds brought trees down in Plumas County, while heavy rains brought local flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind damage was focused on Lake Almanor Peninsula and Lake Almanor West with reports of trees into 3 homes, downed power lines, a propane release and other damages. Major damage was done to one home, minor damage to 2 other homes on the Peninsula.  Three vehicles were damaged including a Sheriff Deputy patrol car. Many residents suffered mud and debris problems in yards and driveways. Highway 32 was closed near the causeway due to a debris flow across the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire started in the Sierra Nevada Mountains at Steep Hollow Creek in the You Bet area, west of Alta, CA on July 25th at approximately 2:37 pm. The fire was not fully contained until August 10th. The cause is still under investigation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were 2304 acres burned, with a total of 6 firefighters injured. Two firefighters from the US Forest Service and 2 from CAL Fire suffered burns while fighting the fire. One of the firefighters had serious burns and required further hospitalization. There were 2 structures destroyed, 1 damaged. Wildfire suppression costs were estimated to be $18 million.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KNF98 RAWS recorded several gusts exceeding 57 mph during this interval.  The peak gust was 63 mph recorded at 18/0320 PST and 18/0420 PST.  The Bolam RAWS recorded a gust to 58 mph at 18/0403 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of storms brought high winds to isolate portions of northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KNF98 RAWS recorded gusts exceeding 58 mph almost continuously during this interval.  The peak gust was 74 mph recorded at 21/0920 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold storm from the Gulf of Alaska brought the first significant snow to the mountains and down into the upper foothills. There were snowfall amounts up to a foot above 6000 ft, with measurable snow down to as low as 2700 feet. This caused travel delays and accidents on mountain roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a 24 hour total of 1.30 inches of rain, snowfall 0.7 inches at Pacific House.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually cold storm brought some snow down as low as the foothills.  While brief, a period of heavy snow impacted holiday travel causing disruptions due to slippery roads. Snow totals in the mountains ranged up to 7 to 10 inches, with some wet snow observed as low as Auburn (1300 feet).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 9.6 inches of snow measured at Mesonet station 9 NNW Twin Bridges.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough developed just off the West Coast on the 2nd and swung through California on the 3rd and 4th, bringing cold, unstable air to southern California with thunderstorms and record rainfall for San Diego. Lindbergh Field recorded a one-day total of 1.09 inches on the 3rd, setting a daily rainfall record. Elsewhere, amounts varied widely with coast and valley locations recording 2-day totals of one-tenth to almost an inch and a half (highest amounts in southern San Diego County). The heavy rain resulted in urban flooding in Spring Valley and Lemon Grove with water up to the doors of some vehicles and several roads closed. Orange County and the Inland Empire received around one-tenth of an inch to one-half of an inch of rain with no flooding. Thunderstorms resulted in several reports of hail one-quarter inch in diameter near Dana Point and in southern San Diego County, but no damage. Strong, gusty west winds also accompanied this event on the 2nd with several gusts in the mountains and desert slopes around 60-65 mph. No wind damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: News media reports flooding near Federal Blvd. and San Miguel Ave in Lemon Grove, with debris also covering the road. Two blocks were impassable with water up to the doors of vehicles on College Ave. off of State Route 94.  Pacific Highway was closed from Juniper to Laurel Streets due to flooding. San Diego County officials reported that Quarry Dip Road was closed between Lakeview Road and State Route 125 in Spring Valley due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough developed along the West Coast, deepening into a strong low over southern California on the 16th, bringing scattered light rain/snow showers. A couple thunderstorms produced one-quarter inch sized hail near Lake Arrowhead (accumulated to around a half-inch depth) and Running Springs in the San Bernardino mountains. Rainfall totals were mostly one-third of an inch or less, with up to one-half of an inch in the mountains, again, highest amounts in San Diego County. The snow level was around 5500 to 6000 feet, with enough moisture to produce around 4 inches of snow in the Big Bear area, with a rain/melted snow total of 1.5 inches. No flooding reported. This system did produce strong and gusty westerly winds with several trees down in San Diego County and power poles down in Orange County, resulting in power outages for several thousand customers. The strong winds brought a large swell and high surf to the Orange and San Diego County coasts as well. The coastal waters experienced low-end gale force winds during this event, with the San Clemente Basin buoy reporting winds gusting from 35 to 41 kt between 1050 pm on the 15th through 950 am on the 16th, and wave heights peaking at 18 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pine trees reported knocked down near Mcauliffe Middle School and damaged power lines by Los Alamitos High School. Power poles were knocked down in Seal Beach resulting in a power outage affecting 4242 customers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough developed along the West Coast, deepening into a strong low over southern California on the 16th, bringing scattered light rain/snow showers. A couple thunderstorms produced one-quarter inch sized hail near Lake Arrowhead (accumulated to around a half-inch depth) and Running Springs in the San Bernardino mountains. Rainfall totals were mostly one-third of an inch or less, with up to one-half of an inch in the mountains, again, highest amounts in San Diego County. The snow level was around 5500 to 6000 feet, with enough moisture to produce around 4 inches of snow in the Big Bear area, with a rain/melted snow total of 1.5 inches. No flooding reported. This system did produce strong and gusty westerly winds with several trees down in San Diego County and power poles down in Orange County, resulting in power outages for several thousand customers. The strong winds brought a large swell and high surf to the Orange and San Diego County coasts as well. The coastal waters experienced low-end gale force winds during this event, with the San Clemente Basin buoy reporting winds gusting from 35 to 41 kt between 1050 pm on the 15th through 950 am on the 16th, and wave heights peaking at 18 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Carlsbad mesonet station (E0995) reported several wind gusts at or above 50 mph between 0000 and 1200 PST on the 16th, including two high wind gusts, 58 mph and 60 mph between 0000 and 0530 PST. The mesonet station at Point Loma Cabrillo reported sustained WNW winds around 40 mph between 0000 and 0430 PST on the 16th, including a peak gust of 58 mph measured twice between 0245-0315 PST.

The California Highway Patrol, Cal Fire-Butte County and Pacific Gas and Electric responded to reports of downed power lines on Dos Rios Road in East Biggs. PG&E���s outage map indicated that about 104 residences were affected by the outage as late as 5 p.m. Sunday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific front moved through interior Northern California bringing rain and mountain snow. Increased shear and instability developed in the afternoon that producing severe storms with large hail and an EF1 tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a tornado with multiple touch downs that moved through the north section of Denair, CA touching down around 1:54 pm PST and ending around 2:15 pm PST on Sunday, November 15, 2015. There were no injuries. About 21 homes were impacted, 2 of them with major damage, but most with minor roof damage. Many fences were knocked down. Numerous trees and tree branches were brought down, including several large trees and numerous sections of trees in walnut groves. Power lines were knocked down causing local power outages. The multiple touch down path was about 3 miles long.  The damage path was about 175-200 yards at the widest point.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN SIERRA SLOPES OF INYO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One man was killed by an avalanche in a remote area of the Sierra just east of the crest. The exact date is unknown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One man was killed by an avalanche in the high Sierra. The exact date and time are unknown; it is believed that the avalanche occurred sometime between the 9th and the 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure just west of the mouth of the Columbia River on the morning of the 24th moved into eastern California and northwest Nevada early on the 25th. This pushed a sharp cold front into northeast California with heavy snow on the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to 6 inches of heavy, wet snow (6-7 to 1 snow to liquid ratio) fell on the 24th south and west of Susanville, including the Sierra Valley and near Bieber. Eleven inches was reported just southeast of Lake Davis at 6000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from the northeast Pacific on the 1st into California and Nevada on the 2nd and 3rd, bringing high elevation (above 8,000 to 9,000 ft) snow with rain changing to snow for lower elevations in the Sierra.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twelve to 18 inches of snow fell near the Sierra crest and in the Sweetwater Range of northern Mono County above 8,500 to 9,000 feet. Lesser amounts of 5 to 11 inches fell down to 7,500 feet, with up to an inch down to 6,500 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure just west of the mouth of the Columbia River on the morning of the 24th moved into eastern California and northwest Nevada early on the 25th. This pushed a sharp cold front into northeast California with heavy snow on the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall of 8 to 15 inches fell from the Truckee airport to along and west of highway 89 on the 24th, with the highest amount in the Tahoe-Donner area. Lesser amounts of 5 to 7 inches fell for most other locations. Areas from Homewood to Glenshire reported 4 inches of snowfall between 1000PST and 1400PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The highest astronomical tides of the year, King Tides, combined with 3 to 4 foot surf to bring minor coastal flooding to low-lying coastal areas of San Diego and Orange Counties during the early mornings on the 23rd and 24th. Areas impacted included Seal Beach in Orange County and La Jolla, Mission Beach, Del Mar, and Imperial Beach in San Diego County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power company reported flooding of patios on Park Avenue, just south of Seal Beach, as well as minor flooding on Pacific Coast Highway and 4 inches of water on Bayview Drive on Sunset Island in Huntington Beach.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold, dry airmass allowed low temperatures to drop into the 20s for much of the Inland Empire and high deserts on the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS station Corona Airport /Ajo/ temperature reached 29 to 32 degrees for a duration of 4 hours. Mesonet station Calimesa (4 s) /up331/. temperature reached 31 to 32 degrees for a duration of 4 hours.  AWOS station March AFB /RIV/ temperature reached 29 to 32 degrees for a duration of 5 hours. Mesonet station Hemet (2 n) /c3539/ temperature reached 32 degrees for a duration of 50 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The highest astronomical tides of the year, King Tides, combined with 3 to 4 foot surf to bring minor coastal flooding to low-lying coastal areas of San Diego and Orange Counties during the early mornings on the 23rd and 24th. Areas impacted included Seal Beach in Orange County and La Jolla, Mission Beach, Del Mar, and Imperial Beach in San Diego County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor tidal flooding occurred in the Midway District in the area of Barnett Ave and Midway Dr., as well as Harbor Drive in Point Loma due to tides overwhelming the storm drains. An NWS employee reported minor tidal flooding of side streets between San Fernando place and San Gabriel place, east of Mission Blvd in south Mission Beach, as well as the curb on the east side of Mission Blvd in that area. The employee also reported minor coastal flooding of Avenida de la Playa in La Jolla,  water, seaweed and foam washing over the sidewalk past the beach barrier wall in La Jolla Shores, and the beach parking lot. The south end of Seacoast Drive in Imperial Beach also saw minor tidal flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continues in full force during the month of December, 2015. Fresno received 2.97 inch of rainfall while Bakersfield only received 0.58 of precipitation during the month of December. Even with these rainfall amount there has been little response in water storage across Central California. ||The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The general trend of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures has continued this year. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. Also, communities in Central California interior face state mandated reductions in municipal water use.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The long term drought continued through November. Widespread precipitation returned to the area with several events, but reservoirs across the area continued to drop well below normal levels. All major reservoirs across the state were 30% or less of capacity. Folsom Lake was down to 14% of capacity, breaking the all-time record low set in 1977. Lake Oroville came close to a record low, but did not reach it.||Extreme drought continued to be detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor across most the region, with exceptional drought for the northern San Joaquin Valley, the central and southern Sacramento Valley, the Burney Basin and the Sierra. ||Governor Jerry Brown declared a drought emergency for the entire state of California January 17, 2014 and this continued to be in effect. He extended the executive order requiring Californians to continue conserving water through October 2016 if drought conditions remain in January 2016.||Local Emergency Proclamations have been issued for El Dorado, Glenn, Lake, Plumas, San Joaquin, Shasta, Sutter, Tuolumne, and Yuba counties. The cities of Live Oak Lodi, and Ripon continue in a drought emergency. Drought task forces have been established in Butte, Lake, Nevada, Placer, Plumas, Sacramento, San Joaquin, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tehama, Tuolumne, and Yolo counties to coordinate response to the drought. ||Mandatory water reductions of 25 percent as ordered by Governor Brown continued to be in effect statewide, the first time such mandatory restrictions have been enacted. ||The state continued to supply food assistance to those impacted by the drought, along with California Disaster Assistance Act money for those who have lost drinking water due to dry wells. Nonprofit organizations in the San Joaquin Valley struggled to meet the needs of their clients for drought-related programs.||Drought stressed and killed many trees in California���s forests, with as much as 20 percent of the state���s forests, or about 120 million trees, potentially dying from drought stress. An earlier survey by the Forest Service revealed an estimated 12.5 million dead trees in the state���s central and southern forests. ||Low runoff in streams and rivers continued to cause adverse affects on fish and other wildlife. This includes the endangered Delta Smelt and winter run Chinook Salmon. The Chinook Salmon lost an estimate 95 percent of hatchlings on the Sacramento River due to the effects of the drought, and may be in danger of extinction. This is also impacting the California salmon fishing industry, which produces an estimated $1.4 billion annually.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Folsom Lake reached a record low volume of 140523 acre-feet on November 14th, breaking the old record set in November 1977. The Household Water Supply Shortage Reporting System had 1 home without well water in Sacramento County, 8 in Yolo County. Wildlife such as the winter-run Chinook Salmon in the Sacramento River suffered catastrophic losses, with the National Marine Fisheries Service reporting in October that 95 percent of the winter-run Chinook eggs, hatchlings and juvenile salmon died this year in the Sacramento River due to changes from the drought, such as an increase in water temperature.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cool early season storm brought wintry weather which impacted travel over Sierra mountain passes. This system also brought rain across the area at lower elevations, locally heavy with thunderstorms. Thunderstorms also caused local damage to power poles from a microburst in Butte County, as well as numerous reports of small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A collapsing thunderstorm resulted in a downburst which brought strong straight line winds which snapped 6 power poles along east Eaton Rd. and Godman Ave in Chico. There were 10000 PG&E customers without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continues in full force during the month of December, 2015. Fresno received 2.97 inch of rainfall while Bakersfield only received 0.58 of precipitation during the month of December. Even with these rainfall amount there has been little response in water storage across Central California. ||The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The general trend of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures has continued this year. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. Also, communities in Central California interior face state mandated reductions in municipal water use.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture and some instability from an easterly wave resulted in scattered thunderstorms over the mountains and lower deserts. Most notable of these was a severe thunderstorm that occurred near Mecca in the southern Coachella Valley during the evening. This storm produced a copious amount of lightning and very strong, damaging winds (over 100 power poles damaged or destroyed). While the precipitation did not hit many gages, the Kent Sea Farms gage, near Mecca, recorded 0.32 inches of rain with this storm. After storm damage surveys, it was concluded that the most likely cause of the damage was a brief, nearly-stationary, EF1 tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The damage was west of Highway 111 between 64th and 66th Avenues, paralleling Highway 86 towards the mountains, or approximately 3 1/2 miles. An emergency manager for the Imperial irrigation water district (IID) reported a total of 140 poles damaged, 30 of which were steel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm from the Gulf of Alaska brought over 2 feet of mountain snow, with lighter amounts into the foothills. Severe thunderstorms developed behind a cold front on the afternoon of the 24th and produced 2 tornadoes, one touching down in El Dorado Hills, the other in Ceres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down at Folsom Lake, and tracked eastward through El Dorado Hills, damaging business and home roofs, and knocking down fences and trees. The tornado continued eastward about 6 miles into Cameron Park, though it was not on the ground continuously. More roofs, trees, and fences were damaged in Cameron Park. No injuries were reported, though a large tree narrowly missed falling on a vehicle with a family of 4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two upper level toughs moved through Southern California between the 10th and 14th of December. The stronger of the two impacted the region from the 12th to the 14th. The troughs produced gusty onshore winds, rain, snow, and a few weak thunderstorms. Precipitation totals for the event ranged from 0.25 to 0.50 inches of rain at the coast to 1.5 to 2.5 inches of rain along the coastal slopes, with up to 6 inches of snow above 7000 ft. A large northwest swell and high astronomical (King) tides also occurred, resulting in some minor coastal flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Volcan Mt. mesonet reported a wind gust of 71 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold storm from the Gulf of Alaska merged with subtropical moisture to bring gusty winds, local road flooding, and heavy snow to the mountains. There were snowfall amounts of 1 to 2 feet above 6000 ft, with measurable snow down to as low as 3400 feet. This caused significant travel delays and accidents on mountain roads. Winds knocked down trees and tree branches causing power outages in the Valley and foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell impacting Lassen Volcanic National Park and the surrounding area into western Plumas County. There were 14 inches of new snow at measured at Manzanita Lake over the weekend. Lighter amounts were measured further east, with 2 inches at Portola and 1 inch at Chester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light winds and wet ground allowed fog to develop overnight and in the early morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 5:20 a.m., 42-year-old male crossed Power Inn Road at Florin Road against the light and was struck by a northbound vehicle that had a green light, according to the CHP. Poor visibility from fog is believed to have been a factor. Speed and alcohol reportedly did not contribute to the crash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold storm from the Gulf of Alaska merged with subtropical moisture to bring heavy snow to the mountains and gusty winds. There were snowfall amounts of 1 to 2 feet above 6000 ft, with measurable snow down to as low as 3200 feet. This caused travel delays and accidents on mountain roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell over the southern Cascades and into the mountains of western Plumas County, with over a foot of snow above 7000 feet. Snow caused travel difficulties on mountain roads. Lassen Volcanic National Park and Hwy 89 were closed on December 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Returning onshore flow ahead of a weak trough brought dense fog to the coast of Orange and San Diego Counties from the evening of December 9th through the morning of the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Los Alamitos U. S. Army Airfield ASOS reported predominately dense fog, with visibility of 1/4 mile or less for 10 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm brought widespread snowfall to northwest California just in time for holiday travel. Snow levels were low. This resulted in chain requirements on many of the area highways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were a few snowfall reports that met warning criteria. The highest snowfall total was eight inches up on Iron Peak near Laytonville. Snow levels were down to as low as the pass levels on Hwy 101 in Mendocino County. CHP contemplated closing Hwy 101 at Ridgewood Summit briefly during the afternoon of 12/24.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm brought widespread snowfall to northwest California just in time for holiday travel. Snow levels were low. This resulted in chain requirements on many of the area highways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were a few snowfall reports that met warning criteria. Snowfall amounts up to a foot were found near South Fork Mountain in western Trinity County. Snow levels were down to as low as the valley floor in Trinity County with chain requirements on Hwy's 3, 36, and 299 ranging from R-1 to R-2. At one point during the day of 12/24, due to heavy snowfall and numerous car and semi-truck spinouts, CHP and Caltrans shut down the pass over Oregon Mountain for 30 minutes to an hour to clear the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm brought widespread snowfall to northwest California just in time for holiday travel. Snow levels were low. This resulted in chain requirements on many of the area highways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous snowfall reports met warning criteria. Snowfall totals of up to 16 inches were reported. Snow levels were down to 1500 to 2000 feet in general. Precipitation initially started off as rain before snow levels lowered. Chain requirements were up for Hwy's 36, 199, and 299, ranging from R-1 to R-2. Hwy 36 was shut down overnight 12/24 to 12/25 at Dinsmore due to heavy snow bringing a tree down over the highway and dragging the power lines down with it. This resulted in a widespread power outage from Bridgeville to Dinsmore. There were multiple CHP reports of vehicle spinouts  and vehicles in the ditch on Hwy 299 near Berry Summit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong north-south pressure gradient generated gusty northerly winds across the Santa Ynez Mountain Range in southern Santa Barbara county. Wind gusts around 60 MPH were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northerly winds developed across southern Santa Barbara county. The RAWS sensor at Montecito reported a wind gust of 60 MPH while the sensor at Las Flores Canyon reported sustained winds of 43 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold northerly flow developed after a cold system moved through the area days prior. This left the region under a fairly cold air mass, which resulted in sub freezing temperatures in the valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Califonia highway department's mesonet station at State Road and Kecks Road reported a minimum temperature of 27 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet and associated trough moved through the area, bringing heavy rain and snow showers. This system also had a good moisture tap which enhanced the precipitation rates and amounts. The strong jet with this system also brought gusty winds as the system began to exit the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The SNOTEL station at Upper Burnt Corral reported a storm snowfall total of 20 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two upper level toughs moved through Southern California between the 10th and 14th of December. The stronger of the two impacted the region from the 12th to the 14th. The troughs produced gusty onshore winds, rain, snow, and a few weak thunderstorms. Precipitation totals for the event ranged from 0.25 to 0.50 inches of rain at the coast to 1.5 to 2.5 inches of rain along the coastal slopes, with up to 6 inches of snow above 7000 ft. A large northwest swell and high astronomical (King) tides also occurred, resulting in some minor coastal flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor coastal flooding of the La Jolla Shores boardwalk. The surf was greater than 7 ft with a high tide near 6.5 feet.
