EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed through the area resulting breezy to gusty winds through the mountain passes. Behind the front a strong low and mid level jet develop, which resulted in some additional gusty winds across the I-5 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 91 MPH wind gust was recorded at the Indian Wells Valley APRS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed through the area resulting breezy to gusty winds through the mountain passes. Behind the front a strong low and mid level jet develop, which resulted in some additional gusty winds across the I-5 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: None thunderstorm wind gust of 73 MPH reported at 926 PM and a 58 MPH at 932 PM. Both of these gust were reported at the Indian Wells Valley APRS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system impacted northwest California during the second week of December bringing a variety of weather and impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A frontal passage brought high winds meeting warning criteria to Crescent City and areas around Humboldt Bay with numerous reports of trees down and power outages. Damage was most widespread from the Arcata Bottoms to Blue Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system impacted northwest California during the second week of December bringing a variety of weather and impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of high astronomical tides, high surf, and elevated water levels in the Navarro River led to the flooding and closure of a portion of Hwy 128 in Mendocino County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system impacted northwest California during the second week of December bringing a variety of weather and impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to a combination of high astronomical tides and high surf, water and debris washed onto some of the area roads. Known roads impacted were Pebble Beach Drive in Crescent City, Highway 101 south of Crescenty City, and Centerville Beach Road near Ferndale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system impacted northwest California during the second week of December bringing a variety of weather and impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to quarter sized hail stones were reported by the public due to a thunderstorm. Photo included coins to reference the hail stone size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest flow and a weak shortwave combined to bring predominantly wind advisory level winds to the mountains and deserts from the 21st to the 22nd of December. A long duration stratiform rain event also occurred, with the bulk of the rain falling from the 21st through the 23rd. Coastal areas received predominantly 0.10 to 0.50 inches of rainfall, while strong upslope flow produced 2 to 4 inches of rainfall along the southwest facing slopes of the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Harrison Park mesonet station reported sustained winds to 51 mph and a wind gust of 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold storm from the Gulf of Alaska merged with subtropical moisture to bring heavy snow to the mountains and gusty winds. There were snowfall amounts of 1 to 2 feet above 6000 ft, with measurable snow down to as low as 3200 feet. This caused travel delays and accidents on mountain roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 1.13 inches of rain over 24 hours, ending at 6 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Returning onshore flow ahead of a weak trough brought dense fog to the coast of Orange and San Diego Counties from the evening of December 9th through the morning of the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fullerton Municipal Airport ASOS reported a prolonged period of dense fog, with visibility of 1/4 mile or less impacting the airport for just over 5 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Returning onshore flow ahead of a weak trough brought dense fog to the coast of Orange and San Diego Counties from the evening of December 9th through the morning of the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The John Wayne Airport ASOS reported a prolonged period of dense fog, with visibility of 1/4 mile or less, that impacted the airport for just over 7 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Returning onshore flow ahead of a weak trough brought dense fog to the coast of Orange and San Diego Counties from the evening of December 9th through the morning of the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The McClellan-Palomar Airport ASOS reported a prolonged period of dense fog, with visibility of 1/4 mile or less impacting the airport for nearly over 12 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Returning onshore flow ahead of a weak trough brought dense fog to the coast of Orange and San Diego Counties from the evening of December 9th through the morning of the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Montgomery Field ASOS reported periods of dense fog, with visibility of 1/4 mile or less over a period of 13 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Returning onshore flow ahead of a weak trough brought dense fog to the coast of Orange and San Diego Counties from the evening of December 9th through the morning of the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Brown Field Municipal Airport ASOS reported intermittent periods of dense fog, with visibility of 1/4 mile or less over a period of 13 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Returning onshore flow ahead of a weak trough brought dense fog to the coast of Orange and San Diego Counties from the evening of December 9th through the morning of the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The North Island Naval Air Station ASOS reported a 4 hour period of dense fog, with visibility of 1/4 mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Returning onshore flow ahead of a weak trough brought dense fog to the coast of Orange and San Diego Counties from the evening of December 9th through the morning of the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The San Diego Lindbergh Field ASOS reported a brief 1 hour period of dense fog, with visibility of 1/4 mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elevated northwest swell brought a low end high surf event to favored beaches from 5th to the 8th of December. The reported impacts were minimal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mission Beach lifeguards reported 7 to 10 ft surf.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure built into the area after a wet rain event. This resulted in areas of fog development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported dense fog, less than 500 feet, on State Road 152 east of Los Banos.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure built into the area after a wet rain event. This resulted in areas of fog development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at KFAT report visibility of 1/16th of a mile at 0039. Also, the AWOS at KFCH reported visibility of less than 1/4 mile at 0135.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at KHJO and KNLC reported visibility of less than 1/4 mile at 2315 and 0003, respectively. Visibility restrictions lasted through the overnight hours beginning around midnight on the 9th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm from the Gulf of Alaska brought over 2 feet of mountain snow, with lighter amounts into the foothills. Severe thunderstorms developed behind a cold front on the afternoon of the 24th and produced 2 tornadoes, one touching down in El Dorado Hills, the other in Ceres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EFO tornado damage was confirmed in Ceres from Davis Rd. to Vinewood Dr. by a NWS damage survey team. A carport collapsed causing damage to a house. Several houses, trees and fences were damaged. The tornado was only on the ground for a brief time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought snow to the mountains, and when combined with gusty winds brought poor visibility at times. There were snowfall amounts up to 15 inches above 7000 ft, causing travel delays on mountain roads. There was minor tree limb damage from winds and some locally heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were snowfall amounts of 12 to 15 inches above 6000 ft, with 6-12 inches above 5000 feet. Snow caused disruption to travel over trans-Sierra highways, including spin-outs, car accidents, jackknifed big rigs, and travel delays, reported by Caltrans.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought snow to the mountains, and when combined with gusty winds brought poor visibility at times. There were snowfall amounts up to 15 inches above 7000 ft, causing travel delays on mountain roads. There was minor tree limb damage from winds and some locally heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were 1.5 inches of heavy rain in Quincy. Gusty winds damaged small limbs.

Nonprofit organizations in the San Joaquin Valley struggled to meet the needs of their clients for drought-related programs. ||Drought stressed and killed many trees in California���s forests, with as much as 20 percent of the state���s forests, or about 120 million trees, potentially dying from drought stress.||Low runoff in streams and rivers continued to cause adverse affects on fish and other wildlife, though there was some improvement in flows from rain events through the month of December. The endangered Delta Smelt and winter run Chinook Salmon were heavily impacted late in the year. This is also impacting the California salmon fishing industry, which produces an estimated $1.4 billion annually.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Household Water Supply Shortage Reporting System indicated there were some homes without well water due to dry wells, with 70 in Butte County, 28 in Glenn County, and 8 in Colusa County. Wildlife such as the winter-run Chinook Salmon in the Sacramento River suffered catastrophic losses, with the National Marine Fisheries Service reporting in October that 95 percent of the winter-run Chinook eggs, hatchlings and juvenile salmon died this year in the Sacramento River due to changes from the drought, such as an increase in water temperature.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure built into the area after a wet rain event. This resulted in areas of fog development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at KMCE, KMAE, and KMER reported visibility of less than 1/4 mile at 1910, 1953, and 2015, respectively. Visibility restrictions lasted through the overnight hours beginning during the evening on the 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold storm from the Gulf of Alaska merged with subtropical moisture to bring gusty winds, local road flooding, and heavy snow to the mountains. There were snowfall amounts of 1 to 2 feet above 6000 ft, with measurable snow down to as low as 3400 feet. This caused significant travel delays and accidents on mountain roads. Winds knocked down trees and tree branches causing power outages in the Valley and foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell impacting the northern Sierra, especially the I80 and Highway 50 corridors. Numerous accidents occurred, and 30 minute hold caused long backups over I80. There were 24 inches of new snow at ski resort peaks, 19 inches at Donner Summit, and 11 inches measured at Kingvale, 7 inches at Blue Canyon, 2.5 inches at Kyburz. Accumulating snow fell down to around 3400 feet, with 1 inch at Pacific House.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold storm from the Gulf of Alaska merged with subtropical moisture to bring gusty winds, local road flooding, and heavy snow to the mountains. There were snowfall amounts of 1 to 2 feet above 6000 ft, with measurable snow down to as low as 3400 feet. This caused significant travel delays and accidents on mountain roads. Winds knocked down trees and tree branches causing power outages in the Valley and foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 1.17 inches of rain measured between 7 am and 4 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold storm from the Gulf of Alaska merged with subtropical moisture to bring heavy snow to the mountains and gusty winds. There were snowfall amounts of 1 to 2 feet above 6000 ft, with measurable snow down to as low as 3200 feet. This caused travel delays and accidents on mountain roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 2.24 inches of rain over 24 hours, ending at 7 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture and some instability from an easterly wave resulted in scattered thunderstorms over the mountains and lower deserts. Most notable of these was a severe thunderstorm that occurred near Mecca in the southern Coachella Valley during the evening. This storm produced a copious amount of lightning and very strong, damaging winds (over 100 power poles damaged or destroyed). While the precipitation did not hit many gages, the Kent Sea Farms gage, near Mecca, recorded 0.32 inches of rain with this storm. After storm damage surveys, it was concluded that the most likely cause of the damage was a brief, nearly-stationary, EF1 tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported two large palm trees fell onto the road at 77th and Harrison, as well as a power pole that fell onto a vehicle near 70th and Pierce Streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front brought high winds to parts of northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Callahan RAWS recorded a peak gust to 59 mph in every observation during this interval.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front brought high winds to parts of northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Montague ASOS reported a gust to 59 mph at 03/0853 PST.  The Weed Airport RAWS recorded a gust to 59 mph at 03/0748 PST and a gust to 60 mph at 03/0848 PST. A member of the public 1NW Montague (estimated) reported that a metal roof was blown off of a barn shed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front brought high winds to parts of northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bolam RAWS recorded gusts exceeding 59 mph continuously from 03/0604 PST until 03/1103 PST.  The peak gust was 70 mph, recorded at 03/0803 PST and 03/1003 PST. The Bolam UPR recorded a gust to 59 mph at 03/0655 PST. The Indian Well RAWS recorded a gust to 61 mph at 03/1001 PST.  The KNF98 portable RAWS recorded gusts exceeding 58 mph continuously between 03/0421 PST and 03/1020 PST. The peak gust was 70 mph recorded at 03/0720 PST. The Van Bremmer RAWS recorded a gust to 60 mph at 03/0931 PST. A spotter at Tennant reported an estimated gust to 60 mph at 03/0953 PST.

Waterfowl in the Delta were significantly impacted by decreased wetlands habitat.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold storm from the Gulf of Alaska merged with subtropical moisture to bring heavy snow to the mountains and gusty winds. There were snowfall amounts of 1 to 2 feet above 6000 ft, with measurable snow down to as low as 3200 feet. This caused travel delays and accidents on mountain roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell over the Sierra, with around 1 to 2 feet above 7000 feet at ski resorts, 7-12 inches between 5000-7000 feet, 1 to 7 inches between 3000-5000 feet. ||There were travel difficulties on highways in the Sierra, such as I-80 and Highway 50. Chain controls were in effect with slowdowns due to periods of whiteout conditions and very slippery roads, and multiple accidents were reported. There was a fatality a little after 5 am on the morning of December 11th, when a driver heading westbound on Highway 50 between Pollock Pines and Placerville lost control and crashed into a tree head-on. It was noted by the CHP that it was snowing at the time of the accident with reduced visibility, and the roadway was wet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold storm from the Gulf of Alaska merged with subtropical moisture to bring gusty winds, local road flooding, and heavy snow to the mountains. There were snowfall amounts of 1 to 2 feet above 6000 ft, with measurable snow down to as low as 3400 feet. This caused significant travel delays and accidents on mountain roads. Winds knocked down trees and tree branches causing power outages in the Valley and foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted up to 61 mph at Redding Airport at 6:24 am PST as a strong cold front moved through. Numerous downed trees with power outages were reported in Tehama and Shasta counties.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Windy conditions occurred over the northern Sacramento Valley over the early morning hours on December 10th, with a peak of 57 mph at Redding Airport at 1:19 am PST. The heavy rain and strong winds across Northern California brought down trees and branches which knocked out power to thousands of customers early Thursday. More than 2,000 customers in Chico lost service. There were also outages reported across Shasta and Tehama counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front brought high winds to parts of northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rush Creek RAWS recorded a gust to 61 mph at 03/1403 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of storms brought high winds to portions of northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KNF98 RAWS recorded numerous gusts exceeding 57 mph during this interval. The peak gust was 71 mph at 05/2320 PST.  The Bolam UPR recorded a gust to 58 mph at 05/2145 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of storms brought high winds to portions of northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KNF98 RAWS recorded a gust to 59 mph at 09/0220 PST and 01/0620 PST. The peak gust was 68 mph recorded at 09/0520 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Post frontal airmass resulted in clearing of skies, light winds, and relatively dry air. This lead to freezing temperatures across the valley. Areas that did not freeze during the overnight developed fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple stations reported a minimum temperature of 27 degrees to include: Harris Ranch, Tranquility, Los Banos, and Firebaugh. Gustine mesonet station reported 26 degrees. San Luis and Santa Nella reported 25 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold low pressure from the North Pacific brought heavy snow to the northern Sierra and down to the lower valleys of northeast California on the 10th and 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell mainly south and west of Susanville on the 10th and early on the 11th, with valleys below 5000 feet starting as rain or a mix of rain and snow before changing over to all snow. Six and a half to 8 inches of snow fell between Sierraville and Lake Davis, including Portola. Ten inches of snow was estimated from the Independence Creek SNOTEL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold low pressure from the North Pacific brought heavy snow to the northern Sierra and down to the lower valleys of northeast California on the 10th and 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A widespread 9 to 16 inches of snow fell around the Tahoe Basin on the 10th and 11th, including 12 to 13 inches in the Tahoe City area and 11 inches about 5 miles southwest of South Lake Tahoe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Sunday, December 13, a cold front swept across the San Francisco Bay Area and Santa Cruz County. Strong winds were observed in the San Francisco Bay Area and Santa Cruz county as the cold front swept through during the late morning hours. Several large trees were blown down, some onto homes and automobiles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large eucalyptus tree was blown down in the city of San Pablo, damaging a home and a car.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large Monterey cypress tree limbs were blown down in the Santa Cruz county community of Rio del Mar. These limbs fell on five houses and damaged at least one vehicle. A branch also tore down a power line, resulting in power outages to 1229 residents.
